rows.pali: 6.Evaṃ anekaguṇasaṅgāhakena sīlasamādhipaññāmukhena desitopi panesa visuddhimaggo atisaṅkhepadesitoyeva hoti.
rows.english: 16.However, even when this path of purification is shown in this way under the headings of virtue, concentration and understanding, each comprising various special qualities, it is still only shown extremely briefly.

rows.pali: Tasmā nālaṃ sabbesaṃ upakārāyāti vitthāramassa dassetuṃ sīlaṃ tāva ārabbha idaṃ pañhākammaṃ hoti.
rows.english: And so since that is insufficient to help all, there is, in order to show it in detail, the following set of questions dealing in the first place with virtue:

rows.pali: Kiṃ sīlaṃ, kenaṭṭhena sīlaṃ, kānassa lakkhaṇarasapaccupaṭṭhānapadaṭṭhānāni, kimānisaṃsaṃ sīlaṃ, katividhaṃ cetaṃ sīlaṃ, ko cassa saṃkileso, kiṃ vodānanti.
rows.english: (i) What is virtue? (ii) In what sense is it virtue? (iii) What are its characteristic, function, manifestation, and proximate cause? (iv) What are the benefits of virtue? (v) How many kinds of virtue are there? (vi) What is the defiling of it? (viii) What is the cleansing of it?

rows.pali: Tatridaṃ vissajjanaṃ.
rows.english: 17.Here are the answers:

rows.pali: Kiṃ sīlanti pāṇātipātādīhi vā viramantassa vattapaṭipattiṃ vā pūrentassa cetanādayo dhammā.
rows.english: (i) WHAT IS VIRTUE? It is the states beginning with volition present in one who abstains from killing living things, etc., or in one who fulfils the practice of the duties.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ paṭisambhidāyaṃ "kiṃ sīlanti cetanā sīlaṃ, cetasikaṃ sīlaṃ, saṃvaro sīlaṃ, avītikkamo sīla"nti (paṭi. ma. 1.39).
rows.english: For this is said in the Paṭisambhidā: “What is virtue? There is virtue as volition, virtue as consciousness-concomitant,7 virtue as restraint, [7] virtue as non- transgression” (Paṭis I 44).

rows.pali: Tattha cetanā sīlaṃ nāma pāṇātipātādīhi vā viramantassa vattapaṭipattiṃ vā pūrentassa cetanā.
rows.english: Herein, virtue as volition is the volition present in one who abstains from killing living things, etc., or in one who fulfils the practice of the duties.

rows.pali: Cetasikaṃ sīlaṃ nāma pāṇātipātādīhi viramantassa virati.
rows.english: Virtue as consciousness- concomitant is the abstinence in one who abstains from killing living things, and so on.

rows.pali: Apica cetanā sīlaṃ nāma pāṇātipātādīni pajahantassa satta kammapathacetanā.
rows.english: Furthermore, virtue as volition is the seven volitions [that accompany the first seven] of the [ten] courses of action (kamma) in one who abandons the killing of living things, and so on.

rows.pali: Cetasikaṃ sīlaṃ nāma "abhijjhaṃ pahāya vigatābhijjhena cetasā viharatī"ti (dī. ni. 1.217) ādinā nayena vuttā anabhijjhābyāpādasammādiṭṭhidhammā.
rows.english: Virtue as consciousness-concomitant is the [three remaining] states consisting of non-covetousness, non-ill will, and right view, stated in the way beginning, “Abandoning covetousness, he dwells with a mind free from covetousness” (D I 71).

rows.pali: Saṃvaro sīlanti ettha pañcavidhena saṃvaro veditabbo pātimokkhasaṃvaro, satisaṃvaro, ñāṇasaṃvaro, khantisaṃvaro, vīriyasaṃvaroti.
rows.english: 18.Virtue as restraint should be understood here as restraint in five ways: restraint by the rules of the community (pātimokkha), restraint by mindfulness, restraint by knowledge, restraint by patience, and restraint by energy.

rows.pali: Tattha iminā pātimokkhasaṃvarena upeto hoti samupetoti (vibha. 511) ayaṃ pātimokkhasaṃvaro.
rows.english: Herein, “restraint by the Pātimokkha” is this: “He is furnished, fully furnished, with this Pātimokkha restraint.(Vibh 246)”

rows.pali: Rakkhati cakkhundriyaṃ, cakkhundriye saṃvaraṃ āpajjatīti (dī. ni. 1.213) ayaṃ satisaṃvaro.
rows.english: “Restraint by mindfulness” is this: “He guards the eye faculty, enters upon restraint of the eye faculty” (D I 70).

rows.pali: Ayaṃ ñāṇasaṃvaro.
rows.english: “Restraint by knowledge” is this (above).

rows.pali: Paccayapaṭisevanampi ettheva samodhānaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: and use of requisites is here combined with this.

rows.pali: Yo panāyaṃ khamo hoti sītassa uṇhassātiādinā (ma. ni. 1.24; a. ni. 6.58) nayena āgato, ayaṃ khantisaṃvaro nāma.
rows.english: But what is called “restraint by patience” is that given in the way beginning, “He is one who bears cold and heat” (M I 10).

rows.pali: Yo cāyaṃ uppannaṃ kāmavitakkaṃ nādhivāsetītiādinā (ma. ni. 1.26; a. ni. 6.58) nayena āgato, ayaṃ vīriyasaṃvaro nāma.
rows.english: And what is called “restraint by energy” is that given in the way beginning, “He does not endure a thought of sense desires when it arises” (M I 11);

rows.pali: Ājīvapārisuddhipi ettheva samodhānaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: purification of livelihood is here combined with this.

rows.pali: Iti ayaṃ pañcavidhopi saṃvaro, yā ca pāpabhīrukānaṃ kulaputtānaṃ sampattavatthuto virati, sabbampetaṃ saṃvarasīlanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: So this fivefold restraint, and the abstinence, in clansmen who dread evil, from any chance of transgression met with, should all be understood to be “virtue as restraint.”

rows.pali: Avītikkamo sīlanti samādinnasīlassa kāyikavācasiko anatikkamo.
rows.english: Virtue as non-transgression is the non-transgression, by body or speech, of precepts of virtue that have been undertaken.

rows.pali: Idaṃ tāva kiṃ sīlanti pañhassa vissajjanaṃ.
rows.english: This, in the first place, is the answer to the question, “What is virtue?

rows.pali: 7.Avasesesu kenaṭṭhena sīlanti sīlanaṭṭhena sīlaṃ.
rows.english: ” [8] Now, as to the rest— 19.(ii) IN WHAT SENSE IS IT VIRTUE? It is virtue (sīla) in the sense of composing (sīlana).8

rows.pali: Kimidaṃ sīlanaṃ nāma.
rows.english: What is this composing?

rows.pali: Samādhānaṃ vā, kāyakammādīnaṃ susīlyavasena avippakiṇṇatāti attho.
rows.english: It is either a coordinating (samādhāna), meaning non- inconsistency of bodily action, etc., due to virtuousness;

rows.pali: Upadhāraṇaṃ vā, kusalānaṃ dhammānaṃ patiṭṭhānavasena ādhārabhāvoti attho.
rows.english: or it is an upholding (upadhāraṇa),8 meaning a state of basis (ādhāra) owing to its serving as foundation for profitable states.

rows.pali: Etadeva hettha atthadvayaṃ saddalakkhaṇavidū anujānanti.
rows.english: For those who understand etymology admit only these two meanings.

rows.pali: Aññe pana siraṭṭho sīlattho, sītalaṭṭho sīlatthoti evamādināpi nayenettha atthaṃ vaṇṇayanti.
rows.english: Others, however, comment on the meaning here in the way beginning, “The meaning of virtue (sīla) is the meaning of head (sira), the meaning of virtue is the meaning of cool (sītala).”

rows.pali: 8.Idāni kānassa lakkhaṇarasapaccupaṭṭhānapadaṭṭhānānīti ettha –
rows.english: 20. (iii) Now, WHAT ARE ITS CHARACTERISTIC, FUNCTION, MANIFESTATION, AND PROXIMATE CAUSE? Here:

rows.pali: Yathā hi nīlapītādibhedena anekadhā bhinnassāpi rūpāyatanassa sanidassanattaṃ lakkhaṇaṃ, nīlādibhedena bhinnassāpi sanidassana bhāvānatikkamanato.
rows.english: Just as visibleness is the characteristic of the visible-data base even when analyzed into the various categories of blue, yellow, etc., because even when analyzed into these categories it does not exceed visible-ness,

rows.pali: Tathā sīlassa cetanādibhedena anekadhā bhinnassāpi yadetaṃ kāyakammādīnaṃ samādhānavasena kusalānañca dhammānaṃ patiṭṭhānavasena vuttaṃ sīlanaṃ, tadeva lakkhaṇaṃ, cetanādibhedena bhinnassāpi samādhānapatiṭṭhānabhāvānatikkamanato.
rows.english: so also this same composing, described above as the coordinating of bodily action, etc., and as the foundation of profitable states, is the characteristic of virtue even when analyzed into the various categories of volition, etc., because even when analyzed into these categories it does not exceed the state of coordination and foundation.

rows.pali: Evaṃ lakkhaṇassa panassa –
rows.english: 21.While such is its characteristic:

rows.pali: Tasmā idaṃ sīlaṃ nāma kiccaṭṭhena rasena dussīlyaviddhaṃsanarasaṃ, sampattiatthena rasena anavajjarasanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: So what is called virtue should be understood to have the function (nature) of stopping misconduct as its function (nature) in the sense of action, and a blameless function (nature) as its function (nature) in the sense of achievement.

rows.pali: Lakkhaṇādīsu hi kiccameva sampatti vā rasoti vuccati.
rows.english: For under [these headings of] characteristic, etc., it is action (kicca) or it is achievement (sampatti) that is called “function” (rasa—nature).

rows.pali: Tayidaṃ sīlaṃ kāyasoceyyaṃ vacīsoceyyaṃ manosoceyyanti (a. ni. 3.121) evaṃ vuttasoceyyapaccupaṭṭhānaṃ, soceyyabhāvena paccupaṭṭhāti gahaṇabhāvaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: This virtue is manifested as the kinds of purity stated thus: “Bodily purity, verbal purity, mental purity” (A I 271); it is manifested, comes to be apprehended, as a pure state.

rows.pali: Hirottappañca panassa viññūhi padaṭṭhānanti vaṇṇitaṃ, āsannakāraṇanti attho.
rows.english: But conscience and shame are said by those who know to be its proximate cause; its near reason, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Hirottappe hi sati sīlaṃ uppajjati ceva tiṭṭhati ca.
rows.english: For when conscience and shame are in existence, virtue arises and persists;

rows.pali: Asati neva uppajjati, na tiṭṭhatīti.
rows.english: and when they are not, it neither arises nor persists.

rows.pali: Evaṃ sīlassa lakkhaṇarasapaccupaṭṭhānapadaṭṭhānāni veditabbāni.
rows.english: This is how virtue’s characteristic, function, manifestation, and proximate cause, should be understood.

rows.pali: 9.Kimānisaṃsaṃ sīlanti avippaṭisārādianekaguṇapaṭilābhānisaṃsaṃ.
rows.english: 23.(iv) WHAT ARE THE BENEFITS OF VIRTUE? Its benefits are the acquisition of the several special qualities beginning with non-remorse.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ – "avippaṭisāratthāni kho, ānanda, kusalāni sīlāni avippaṭisārānisaṃsānī"ti (a. ni. 11.1).
rows.english: For this is said: “Ānanda, profitable habits (virtues) have non-remorse as their aim and non-remorse as their benefit” (A V 1).

rows.pali: Aparampi vuttaṃ "pañcime gahapatayo ānisaṃsā sīlavato sīlasampadāya.
rows.english: Also it is said further: “Householder, there are these five benefits for the virtuous in the perfecting of virtue.

rows.pali: Katame pañca?
rows.english: What five?

rows.pali: Idha gahapatayo sīlavā sīlasampanno appamādādhikaraṇaṃ mahantaṃ bhogakkhandhaṃ adhigacchati, ayaṃ paṭhamo ānisaṃso sīlavato sīlasampadāya.
rows.english: Here, householder, one who is virtuous, possessed of virtue, obtains a large fortune as a consequence of diligence; this is the first benefit for the virtuous in the perfecting of virtue.

rows.pali: Puna caparaṃ gahapatayo sīlavato sīlasampannassa kalyāṇo kittisaddo abbhuggacchati, ayaṃ dutiyo ānisaṃso sīlavato sīlasampadāya.
rows.english: Again, of one who is virtuous, possessed of virtue, a fair name is spread abroad; this is the second benefit for the virtuous in the perfecting of virtue.

rows.pali: Puna caparaṃ gahapatayo sīlavā sīlasampanno yaññadeva parisaṃ upasaṅkamati yadi khattiyaparisaṃ yadi brāhmaṇaparisaṃ yadi gahapatiparisaṃ yadi samaṇaparisaṃ, visārado upasaṅkamati amaṅkubhūto, ayaṃ tatiyo ānisaṃso sīlavato sīlasampadāya.
rows.english: Again, whenever one who is virtuous, possessed of virtue, enters an assembly, whether of khattiyas (warrior- nobles) or brahmans or householders or ascetics, he does so without fear or hesitation; this is the third benefit for the virtuous in the perfecting of virtue.

rows.pali: Puna caparaṃ gahapatayo sīlavā sīlasampanno asammūḷho kālaṃ karoti, ayaṃ catuttho ānisaṃso sīlavato sīlasampadāya.
rows.english: Again, one who is virtuous, possessed of virtue, dies unconfused; this is the fourth benefit for the virtuous in the perfecting of virtue.

rows.pali: Puna caparaṃ gahapatayo sīlavā sīlasampanno kāyassa bhedā paraṃ maraṇā sugatiṃ saggaṃ lokaṃ upapajjati, ayaṃ pañcamo ānisaṃso sīlavato sīlasampadāyā"ti (dī. ni. 2.150; a. ni. 5.213; mahāva. 285).
rows.english: Again, one who is virtuous, possessed of virtue, on the breakup of the body, after death, reappears in a happy destiny, in the heavenly world; this is the fifth benefit for the virtuous in the perfecting of virtue” (D II 86).

rows.pali: Aparepi "ākaṅkheyya ce, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabrahmacārīnaṃ piyo ca assaṃ manāpo ca garu ca bhāvanīyo cāti, sīlesvevassa paripūrakārī"tiādinā (ma. ni. 1.65) nayena piyamanāpatādayo āsavakkhayapariyosānā anekā sīlānisaṃsā vuttā.
rows.english: There are also the many benefits of virtue beginning with being dear and loved and ending with destruction of cankers described in the passage beginning, “If a bhikkhu should wish, ‘May I be dear to my fellows in the life of purity and loved by them, held in respect and honoured by them,’ let him perfect the virtues” (M I 33).

rows.pali: Evaṃ avippaṭisārādianekaguṇānisaṃsaṃ sīlaṃ.
rows.english: This is how virtue has as its benefits the several special qualities beginning with non-remorse.

rows.pali: Apica –
rows.english: 24.Furthermore:

rows.pali: 10.Idāni yaṃ vuttaṃ katividhaṃ cetaṃ sīlanti, tatridaṃ vissajjanaṃ.
rows.english: 25. (v) Now, here is the answer to the question, HOW MANY KINDS OF VIRTUE ARE THERE?

rows.pali: Sabbameva tāva idaṃ sīlaṃ attano sīlanalakkhaṇena ekavidhaṃ.
rows.english: 1.Firstly all this virtue is of one kind by reason of its own characteristic of composing.

rows.pali: Cārittavārittavasena duvidhaṃ.
rows.english: 2.It is of two kinds as keeping and avoiding.

rows.pali: Tathā ābhisamācārikaādibrahmacariyakavasena, viratiavirativasena, nissitānissitavasena, kālapariyantaāpāṇakoṭikavasena, sapariyantāpariyantavasena, lokiyalokuttaravasena ca.
rows.english: 3.Likewise as that of good behaviour and that of the beginning of the life of purity, 4.As abstinence and non-abstinence, 5.As dependent and independent, 6.As temporary and lifelong, 7.As limited and unlimited, 8.As mundane and supramundane.

rows.pali: Tividhaṃ hīnamajjhimapaṇītavasena.
rows.english: 9.It is of three kinds as inferior, medium, and superior.

rows.pali: Tathā attādhipateyyalokādhipateyyadhammādhipateyyavasena, parāmaṭṭhāparāmaṭṭhapaṭippassaddhivasena, visuddhāvisuddhavematikavasena, sekkhāsekkhanevasekkhanāsekkhavasena ca.
rows.english: 10.Likewise as giving precedence to self, giving precedence to the world, and giving precedence to the Dhamma, 11.As adhered to, not adhered to, and tranquillized. 12.As purified, unpurified, and dubious. 13.As that of the trainer, that of the non-trainer, and that of the neither-trainer- nor-non-trainer.

rows.pali: Catubbidhaṃ hānabhāgiyaṭhitibhāgiyavisesabhāgiyanibbedhabhāgiyavasena.
rows.english: 14.It is of four kinds as partaking of diminution, of stagnation, of distinction, of penetration.

rows.pali: Tathā bhikkhubhikkhunīanupasampannagahaṭṭhasīlavasena, pakatiācāradhammatāpubbahetukasīlavasena, pātimokkhasaṃvaraindriyasaṃvaraājīvapārisuddhipaccayasannissitasīlavasena ca.
rows.english: 15.Likewise as that of bhikkhus, of bhikkhunīs, of the not-fully-admitted, of the laity, 16.As natural, customary, necessary, due to previous causes, 17.As virtue of Pātimokkha restraint, of restraint of sense faculties, of purification of livelihood, and that concerning requisites.

rows.pali: Pañcavidhaṃpariyantapārisuddhisīlādivasena.
rows.english: 18.It is of five kinds as virtue consisting in limited purification, etc.;

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ paṭisambhidāyaṃ "pañca sīlāni – pariyantapārisuddhisīlaṃ, apariyantapārisuddhisīlaṃ, paripuṇṇapārisuddhisīlaṃ, aparāmaṭṭhapārisuddhisīlaṃ, paṭippassaddhipārisuddhisīla"nti (paṭi. ma. 1.37).
rows.english: for this is said in the Paṭisambhidā: “Five kinds of virtue: virtue consisting in limited purification, virtue consisting in unlimited purification, virtue consisting in fulfilled purification, virtue consisting in unadhered-to purification, virtue consisting in tranquillized purification” (Paṭis I 42).

rows.pali: Tathā pahānaveramaṇīcetanāsaṃvarāvītikkamavasena.
rows.english: 19.Likewise as abandoning, refraining, volition, restraint, and non-transgression.

rows.pali: 11.Tattha ekavidhakoṭṭhāse attho vuttanayeneva veditabbo.
rows.english: 26. 1. Herein, in the section dealing with that of one kind, the meaning should be understood as already stated.

rows.pali: Duvidhakoṭṭhāse yaṃ bhagavatā "idaṃ kattabba"nti paññattasikkhāpadapūraṇaṃ, taṃ cārittaṃ.
rows.english: 2. In the section dealing with that of two kinds: fulfilling a training precept announced by the Blessed One thus: “This should be done” is keeping;

rows.pali: Yaṃ "idaṃ na kattabba"nti paṭikkhittassa akaraṇaṃ, taṃ vārittaṃ.
rows.english: not doing what is prohibited by him thus: “This should not be done” is avoiding.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ vacanattho.
rows.english: Herein, the word- meaning is this:

rows.pali: Caranti tasmiṃ sīlesu paripūrakāritāya pavattantīti cārittaṃ.
rows.english: they keep (caranti) within that, they proceed as people who fulfil the virtues, thus it is keeping (cāritta);

rows.pali: Vāritaṃ tāyanti rakkhanti tenāti vārittaṃ.
rows.english: they preserve, they protect, they avoid, thus it is avoiding.

rows.pali: Tattha saddhāvīriyasādhanaṃ cārittaṃ, saddhāsādhanaṃ vārittaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, keeping is accomplished by faith and energy; avoiding, by faith and mindfulness.

rows.pali: Evaṃ cārittavārittavasena duvidhaṃ.
rows.english: This is how it is of two kinds as keeping and avoiding.

rows.pali: Dutiyaduke abhisamācāroti uttamasamācāro.
rows.english: 27. 3. In the second dyad good behaviour is the best kind of behaviour.

rows.pali: Abhisamācāro eva ābhisamācārikaṃ.
rows.english: Good behaviour itself is that of good behaviour;

rows.pali: Abhisamācāraṃ vā ārabbha paññattaṃ ābhisamācārikaṃ, ājīvaṭṭhamakato avasesasīlassetaṃ adhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: or what is announced for the sake of good behaviour is that of good behaviour.

rows.pali: Maggabrahmacariyassa ādibhāvabhūtanti ādibrahmacariyakaṃ, ājīvaṭṭhamakasīlassetaṃ adhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: This is a term for virtue other than that which has livelihood as eighth.9 It is the initial stage of the life of purity consisting in the path, thus it is that of the beginning of the life of purity. This is a term for the virtue that has livelihood as eighth.

rows.pali: Tañhi maggassa ādibhāvabhūtaṃ, pubbabhāgeyeva parisodhetabbato.
rows.english: It is the initial stage of the path because it has actually to be purified in the prior stage too.

rows.pali: Tenāha – "pubbeva kho panassa kāyakammaṃ vacīkammaṃ ājīvo suparisuddho hotī"ti (ma. ni. 3.431).
rows.english: Hence it is said: “But his bodily action, his verbal action, and his livelihood have already been purified earlier” (M III 289).

rows.pali: Yāni vā sikkhāpadāni khuddānukhuddakānīti vuttāni, idaṃ ābhisamācārikasīlaṃ.
rows.english: Or the training precepts called “lesser and minor” (D II 154) are that of good behaviour;

rows.pali: Sesaṃ ādibrahmacariyakaṃ.
rows.english: the rest are that of the beginning of the life of purity.

rows.pali: Ubhatovibhaṅgapariyāpannaṃ vā ādibrahmacariyakaṃ.
rows.english: Or what is included in the Double Code (the bhikkhus’ and bhikkhunīs’ Pātimokkha) is that of the beginning of the life of purity;

rows.pali: Khandhakavattapariyāpannaṃ ābhisamācārikaṃ.
rows.english: and that included in the duties set out in the Khandhakas [of Vinaya] is that of good behaviour.

rows.pali: Tassa sampattiyā ādibrahmacariyakaṃ sampajjati.
rows.english: Through its perfection that of the beginning of the life of purity comes to be perfected.

rows.pali: Tenevāha – "so vata, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ābhisamācārikaṃ dhammaṃ aparipūretvā ādibrahmacariyakaṃ dhammaṃ paripūressatīti netaṃ ṭhānaṃ vijjatī"ti (a. ni. 5.21).
rows.english: Hence it is said also “that this bhikkhu shall fulfil the state consisting in the beginning of the life of purity without having fulfilled the state consisting in good behaviour—that is not possible” (A III 14–15).

rows.pali: Evaṃ ābhisamācārikaādibrahmacariyakavasena duvidhaṃ.
rows.english: So it is of two kinds as that of good behaviour and that of the beginning of the life of purity.

rows.pali: Tatiyaduke pāṇātipātādīhi veramaṇimattaṃ viratisīlaṃ.
rows.english: 28.4.In the third dyad virtue as abstinence is simply abstention from killing living things, etc.;

rows.pali: Sesaṃ cetanādi aviratisīlanti evaṃ viratiavirativasena duvidhaṃ.
rows.english: the other kinds consisting in volition, etc., are virtue as non-abstinence. So it is of two kinds as abstinence and non-abstinence.

rows.pali: Catutthaduke nissayoti dve nissayā taṇhānissayo ca diṭṭhinissayo ca.
rows.english: 29.5.In the fourth dyad there are two kinds of dependence: dependence through craving and dependence through [false] views.

rows.pali: Tattha yaṃ "imināhaṃ sīlena devo vā bhavissāmi devaññataro vā"ti (dī. ni. 3.320; ma. ni. 1.186; a. ni. 5.206; 7.50) evaṃ bhavasampattiṃ ākaṅkhamānena pavattitaṃ, idaṃ taṇhānissitaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, that produced by one who wishes for a fortunate kind of becoming thus, “Through this virtuous conduct [rite] I shall become a [great] deity or some [minor] deity” (M I 102), is dependent through craving.

rows.pali: Yaṃ "sīlena suddhī"ti evaṃ suddhidiṭṭhiyā pavattitaṃ, idaṃ diṭṭhinissitaṃ.
rows.english: That produced through such [false] view about purification as “Purification is through virtuous conduct” (Vibh 374) is dependent through [false] view.

rows.pali: Yaṃ pana lokuttaraṃ lokiyañca tasseva sambhārabhūtaṃ, idaṃ anissitanti evaṃ nissitānissitavasena duvidhaṃ.
rows.english: But the supramundane, and the mundane that is the prerequisite for the aforesaid supramundane, are independent. So it is of two kinds as dependent and independent.

rows.pali: Pañcamaduke kālaparicchedaṃ katvā samādinnaṃ sīlaṃ kālapariyantaṃ.
rows.english: 30. 6. In the fifth dyad temporary virtue is that undertaken after deciding on a time limit.

rows.pali: Yāvajīvaṃ samādiyitvā tatheva pavattitaṃ āpāṇakoṭikanti evaṃ kālapariyantaāpāṇakoṭikavasena duvidhaṃ.
rows.english: Lifelong virtue is that practiced in the same way but undertaking it for as long as life lasts. So it is of two kinds as temporary and lifelong.

rows.pali: Chaṭṭhaduke lābhayasañātiaṅgajīvitavasena diṭṭhapariyantaṃ sapariyantaṃ nāma.
rows.english: 31.7.In the sixth dyad the limited is that seen to be limited by gain, fame, relatives, limbs, or life.

rows.pali: Viparītaṃ apariyantaṃ.
rows.english: The opposite is unlimited.

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ paṭisambhidāyaṃ "katamaṃ taṃ sīlaṃ sapariyantaṃ?
rows.english: And this is said in the Paṭisambhidā: “What is the virtue that has a limit?

rows.pali: Atthi sīlaṃ lābhapariyantaṃ, atthi sīlaṃ yasapariyantaṃ, atthi sīlaṃ ñātipariyantaṃ, atthi sīlaṃ aṅgapariyantaṃ, atthi sīlaṃ jīvitapariyantaṃ.
rows.english: There is virtue that has gain as its limit, there is virtue that has fame as its limit, there is virtue that has relatives as its limit, there is virtue that has limbs as its limit, there is virtue that has life as its limit.

rows.pali: Katamaṃ taṃ sīlaṃ lābhapariyantaṃ?
rows.english: What is virtue that has gain as its limit?

rows.pali: Idhekacco lābhahetu lābhapaccayā lābhakāraṇā yathāsamādinnaṃ sikkhāpadaṃ vītikkamati, idaṃ taṃ sīlaṃ lābhapariyanta"nti (paṭi. ma. 1.38).
rows.english: Here someone with gain as cause, with gain as condition, with gain as reason, transgresses a training precept as undertaken: that virtue has gain as its limit” (Paṭis I 43),

rows.pali: Eteneva upāyena itarānipi vitthāretabbāni.
rows.english: and the rest should be elaborated in the same way.

rows.pali: Apariyantavissajjanepi vuttaṃ "katamaṃ taṃ sīlaṃ na lābhapariyantaṃ?
rows.english: Also in the answer dealing with the unlimited it is said: “What is virtue that does not have gain as its limit?

rows.pali: Idhekacco lābhahetu lābhapaccayā lābhakāraṇā yathāsamādinnaṃ sikkhāpadaṃ vītikkamāya cittampi na uppādeti, kiṃ so vītikkamissati, idaṃ taṃ sīlaṃ na lābhapariyanta"nti (paṭi. ma. 1.38).
rows.english: Here someone does not, with gain as cause, with gain as condition, with gain as reason, even arouse the thought of transgressing a training precept as undertaken, how then shall he actually transgress it? That virtue does not have gain as its limit” (Paṭis I 44),

rows.pali: Etenevupāyena itarānipi vitthāretabbāni.
rows.english: and the rest should be elaborated in the same way.

rows.pali: Evaṃ sapariyantāpariyantavasena duvidhaṃ.
rows.english: So it is of two kinds as limited and unlimited.

rows.pali: Sattamaduke sabbampi sāsavaṃ sīlaṃ lokiyaṃ.
rows.english: 32.8.In the seventh dyad all virtue subject to cankers is mundane;

rows.pali: Anāsavaṃ lokuttaraṃ.
rows.english: that not subject to cankers is supramundane.

rows.pali: Tattha lokiyaṃ bhavavisesāvahaṃ hoti bhavanissaraṇassa ca sambhāro.
rows.english: Herein, the mundane brings about improvement in future becoming and is a prerequisite for the escape from becoming,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "vinayo saṃvaratthāya, saṃvaro avippaṭisāratthāya, avippaṭisāro pāmojjatthāya, pāmojjaṃ pītatthāya, pīti passaddhatthāya, passaddhi sukhatthāya, sukhaṃ samādhatthāya, samādhi yathābhūtañāṇadassanatthāya, yathābhūtañāṇadassanaṃ nibbidatthāya, nibbidā virāgatthāya, virāgo vimuttatthāya, vimutti vimuttiñāṇadassanatthāya, vimuttiñāṇadassanaṃ anupādāparinibbānatthāya, etadatthā kathā, etadatthā mantanā, etadatthā upanisā, etadatthaṃ sotāvadhānaṃ, yadidaṃ anupādācittassa vimokkho"ti (pari. 366).
rows.english: according as it is said: “Discipline is for the purpose of restraint, restraint is for the purpose of non- remorse, non-remorse is for the purpose of gladdening, gladdening is for the purpose of happiness, happiness is for the purpose of tranquillity, tranquillity is for the purpose of bliss, bliss is for the purpose of concentration, concentration is for the purpose of correct knowledge and vision, correct knowledge and vision is for the purpose of dispassion, dispassion is for the purpose of fading away [of greed], fading away is for the purpose of deliverance, deliverance is for the purpose of knowledge and vision of deliverance, knowledge and vision of deliverance is for the purpose of complete extinction [of craving, etc.] through not clinging. Talk has that purpose, counsel has that purpose, support has that purpose, giving ear has that purpose, that is to say, the liberation of the mind through not clinging” (Vin V 164).

rows.pali: Lokuttaraṃ bhavanissaraṇāvahaṃ hoti paccavekkhaṇañāṇassa ca bhūmīti evaṃ lokiyalokuttaravasena duvidhaṃ.
rows.english: The supramundane brings about the escape from becoming and is the plane of reviewing knowledge. So it is of two kinds as mundane and supramundane.

rows.pali: 12.Tikesu paṭhamattike hīnena chandena cittena vīriyena vīmaṃsāya vā pavattitaṃ hīnaṃ.
rows.english: 33. 9. In the first of the triads the inferior is produced by inferior zeal, [purity of] consciousness, energy, or inquiry;

rows.pali: Majjhimehi chandādīhi pavattitaṃ majjhimaṃ.
rows.english: the medium is produced by medium zeal, etc.;

rows.pali: Paṇītehi paṇītaṃ.
rows.english: the superior, by superior (zeal, and so on).

rows.pali: Yasakāmatāya vā samādinnaṃ hīnaṃ.
rows.english: That undertaken out of desire for fame is inferior;

rows.pali: Puññaphalakāmatāya majjhimaṃ.
rows.english: that undertaken out of desire for the fruits of merit is medium;

rows.pali: Kattabbamevidanti ariyabhāvaṃ nissāya samādinnaṃ paṇītaṃ.
rows.english: that undertaken for the sake of the noble state thus, “This has to be done” is superior.

rows.pali: "Ahamasmi sīlasampanno, ime panaññe bhikkhū dussīlā pāpadhammā"ti evaṃ attukkaṃsanaparavambhanādīhi upakkiliṭṭhaṃ vā hīnaṃ.
rows.english: Or again, that defiled by self-praise and disparagement of others, etc., thus, “I am possessed of virtue, but these other bhikkhus are ill-conducted and evil-natured” (M I 193), is inferior;

rows.pali: Anupakkiliṭṭhaṃ lokiyaṃ sīlaṃ majjhimaṃ.
rows.english: undefiled mundane virtue is medium;

rows.pali: Lokuttaraṃ paṇītaṃ.
rows.english: supramundane is superior.

rows.pali: Taṇhāvasena vā bhavabhogatthāya pavattitaṃ hīnaṃ.
rows.english: Or again, that motivated by craving, the purpose of which is to enjoy continued existence, is inferior;

rows.pali: Attano vimokkhatthāya pavattitaṃ majjhimaṃ.
rows.english: that practiced for the purpose of one’s own deliverance is medium;

rows.pali: Sabbasattānaṃ vimokkhatthāya pavattitaṃ pāramitāsīlaṃ paṇītanti evaṃ hīnamajjhimapaṇītavasena tividhaṃ.
rows.english: the virtue of the perfections practiced for the deliverance of all beings is superior. So it is of three kinds as inferior, medium, and superior.

rows.pali: Dutiyattike attano ananurūpaṃ pajahitukāmena attagarunā attanigāravena pavattitaṃ attādhipateyyaṃ.
rows.english: 34.10. In the second triad that practiced out of self-regard by one who regards self and desires to abandon what is unbecoming to self is virtue giving precedence to self.

rows.pali: Lokāpavādaṃ pariharitukāmena lokagarunā loke gāravena pavattitaṃ lokādhipateyyaṃ.
rows.english: That practiced out of regard for the world and out of desire to ward off the censure of the world is virtue giving precedence to the world.

rows.pali: Dhammamahattaṃ pūjetukāmena dhammagarunā dhammagāravena pavattitaṃ dhammādhipateyyanti evaṃ attādhipateyyādivasena tividhaṃ.
rows.english: That practiced out of regard for the Dhamma and out of desire to honour the majesty of the Dhamma is virtue giving precedence to the Dhamma. So it is of three kinds as giving precedence to self, and so on.

rows.pali: Tatiyattike yaṃ dukesu nissitanti vuttaṃ, taṃ taṇhādiṭṭhīhi parāmaṭṭhattā parāmaṭṭhaṃ.
rows.english: 35.11. In the third triad the virtue that in the dyads was called dependent (no. 5) is adhered-to because it is adhered-to through craving and [false] view.

rows.pali: Puthujjanakalyāṇakassa maggasambhārabhūtaṃ sekkhānañca maggasampayuttaṃ aparāmaṭṭhaṃ.
rows.english: That practiced by the magnanimous ordinary man as the prerequisite of the path, and that associated with the path in trainers, are not-adhered-to.

rows.pali: Sekkhāsekkhānaṃ phalasampayuttaṃ paṭippassaddhanti evaṃ parāmaṭṭhādivasena tividhaṃ.
rows.english: That associated with trainers’ and non-trainers’ fruition is tranquillized. So it is of three kinds as adhered-to, and so on.

rows.pali: Catutthattike yaṃ āpattiṃ anāpajjantena pūritaṃ, āpajjitvā vā puna katapaṭikammaṃ, taṃ visuddhaṃ.
rows.english: 36.12. In the fourth triad that fulfilled by one who has committed no offence or has made amends after committing one is pure.

rows.pali: Āpattiṃ āpannassa akatapaṭikammaṃ avisuddhaṃ.
rows.english: So long as he has not made amends after committing an offence it is impure.

rows.pali: Vatthumhi vā āpattiyā vā ajjhācāre vā vematikassa sīlaṃ vematikasīlaṃ nāma.
rows.english: Virtue in one who is dubious about whether a thing constitutes an offence or about what grade of offence has been committed or about whether he has committed an offence is dubious.

rows.pali: Tattha yoginā avisuddhasīlaṃ visodhetabbaṃ, vematike vatthujjhācāraṃ akatvā vimati paṭivinetabbā "iccassa phāsu bhavissatī"ti evaṃ visuddhādivasena tividhaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, the meditator should purify impure virtue. If dubious, he should avoid cases about which he is doubtful and should get his doubts cleared up. In this way his mind will be kept at rest. So it is of three kinds as pure, and so on.

rows.pali: Pañcamattike catūhi ariyamaggehi tīhi ca sāmaññaphalehi sampayuttaṃ sīlaṃ sekkhaṃ.
rows.english: 37.13. In the fifth triad the virtue associated with the four paths and with the [first] three fruitions is that of the trainer.

rows.pali: Arahattaphalasampayuttaṃ asekkhaṃ.
rows.english: That associated with the fruition of Arahantship is that of the non-trainer.

rows.pali: Sesaṃ nevasekkhanāsekkhanti evaṃ sekkhādivasena tividhaṃ.
rows.english: The remaining kinds are that of the neither- trainer-nor-non-trainer. So it is of three kinds as that of the trainer, and so on.

rows.pali: Paṭisambhidāyaṃ pana yasmā loke tesaṃ tesaṃ sattānaṃ pakatipi sīlanti vuccati, yaṃ sandhāya "ayaṃ sukhasīlo, ayaṃ dukkhasīlo, ayaṃ kalahasīlo, ayaṃ maṇḍanasīlo"ti bhaṇanti, tasmā tena pariyāyena "tīṇi sīlāni, kusalasīlaṃ akusalasīlaṃ abyākatasīlanti (paṭi. ma. 1.39).
rows.english: 38.But in the world the nature of such and such beings is called their “habit” (sīla) of which they say: “This one is of happy habit (sukha-sīla), this one is of unhappy habit, this one is of quarrelsome habit, this one is of dandified habit.” Because of that it is said in the Paṭisambhidā figuratively: “Three kinds of virtue (habit): profitable virtue, unprofitable virtue, indeterminate virtue” (Paṭis I 44).

rows.pali: Evaṃ kusalādivasenapi tividhanti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: So it is also called of three kinds as profitable, and so on.

rows.pali: Tattha akusalaṃ imasmiṃ atthe adhippetassa sīlassa lakkhaṇādīsu ekenapi na sametīti idha na upanītaṃ, tasmā vuttanayenevassa tividhatā veditabbā.
rows.english: Of these, the unprofitable is not included here since it has nothing whatever to do with the headings beginning with the characteristic, which define virtue in the sense intended in this [chapter]. So the threefoldness should be understood only in the way already stated.

rows.pali: 13.Catukkesu paṭhamacatukke –
rows.english: 39.14. In the first of the tetrads:

rows.pali: Evaṃ hānabhāgiyādivasena catubbidhaṃ.
rows.english: So it is of four kinds as partaking of diminution, and so on.

rows.pali: Dutiyacatukke bhikkhū ārabbha paññattasikkhāpadāni, yāni ca nesaṃ bhikkhunīnaṃ paññattito rakkhitabbāni, idaṃ bhikkhusīlaṃ.
rows.english: 40.15. In the second tetrad there are training precepts announced for bhikkhus to keep irrespective of what is announced for bhikkhunīs. This is the virtue of bhikkhus.

rows.pali: Bhikkhuniyo ārabbha paññattasikkhāpadāni, yāni ca tāsaṃ bhikkhūnaṃ paññattito rakkhitabbāni, idaṃ bhikkhunisīlaṃ.
rows.english: There are training precepts announced for bhikkhunīs to keep irrespective of what is announced for bhikkhus. This is the virtue of bhikkhunīs.

rows.pali: Sāmaṇerasāmaṇerīnaṃ dasasīlāni anupasampannasīlaṃ.
rows.english: The ten precepts of virtue for male and female novices are the virtue of the not fully admitted.

rows.pali: Upāsakaupāsikānaṃ niccasīlavasena pañcasikkhāpadāni, sati vā ussāhe dasa, uposathaṅgavasena aṭṭhāti idaṃ gahaṭṭhasīlanti evaṃ bhikkhusīlādivasena catubbidhaṃ.
rows.english: The five training precepts—ten when possible—as a permanent undertaking and eight as the factors of the Uposatha Day,10 for male and female lay followers are the virtue of the laity. So it is of four kinds as the virtue of bhikkhus, and so on.

rows.pali: Tatiyacatukke uttarakurukānaṃ manussānaṃ avītikkamo pakatisīlaṃ.
rows.english: 41.16. In the third tetrad the non-transgression on the part of Uttarakuru human beings is natural virtue.

rows.pali: Kuladesapāsaṇḍānaṃ attano attano mariyādācārittaṃ ācārasīlaṃ.
rows.english: Each clan’s or locality’s or sect’s own rules of conduct are customary virtue.

rows.pali: "Dhammatā esā, ānanda, yadā bodhisatto mātukucchiṃ okkanto hoti na bodhisattamātu purisesu mānasaṃ uppajji kāmaguṇūpasaṃhita"nti evaṃ vuttaṃ bodhisattamātusīlaṃ dhammatāsīlaṃ.
rows.english: The virtue of the Bodhisatta’s mother described thus: “It is the necessary rule, Ānanda, that when the Bodhisatta has descended into his mother’s womb, no thought of men that is connected with the cords of sense desire comes to her” (D II 13), is necessary virtue.

rows.pali: Mahākassapādīnaṃ pana suddhasattānaṃ, bodhisattassa ca tāsu tāsu jātīsu sīlaṃ pubbahetukasīlanti evaṃ pakatisīlādivasena catubbidhaṃ.
rows.english: But the virtue of such pure beings as Mahā Kassapa, etc., and of the Bodhisatta in his various births is virtue due to previous causes. So it is of four kinds as natural virtue, and so on.

rows.pali: Catutthacatukke yaṃ bhagavatā "idha bhikkhu pātimokkhasaṃvarasaṃvuto viharati ācāragocarasampanno aṇumattesu vajjesu bhayadassāvī samādāya sikkhati sikkhāpadesū"ti (vibha. 508; dī. ni. 1.193) vaṃ vuttaṃ sīlaṃ, idaṃ pātimokkhasaṃvarasīlaṃ nāma.
rows.english: 42.17. In the fourth tetrad: (a) The virtue described by the Blessed One thus: “Here a bhikkhu dwells restrained with the Pātimokkha restraint, possessed of the [proper] conduct and resort, and seeing fear in the slightest fault, he trains himself by undertaking the precepts of training, (Vibh 244)” is virtue of Pātimokkha restraint.

rows.pali: Yaṃ pana "so cakkhunā rūpaṃ disvā na nimittaggāhī hoti nānubyañjanaggāhī, yatvādhikaraṇamenaṃ cakkhundriyaṃ asaṃvutaṃ viharantaṃ abhijjhādomanassā pāpakā akusalā dhammā anvāssaveyyuṃ, tassa saṃvarāya paṭipajjati, rakkhati cakkhundriyaṃ, cakkhundriye saṃvaraṃ āpajjati.
rows.english: (b) That described thus: “On seeing a visible object with the eye, he apprehends neither the signs nor the particulars through which, if he left the eye faculty unguarded, evil and unprofitable states of covetousness and grief might invade him; he enters upon the way of its restraint, he guards the eye faculty, undertakes the restraint of the eye faculty.

rows.pali: Sotena saddaṃ sutvā - pe - ghānena gandhaṃ ghāyitvā - pe - jivhāya rasaṃ sāyitvā - pe - kāyena phoṭṭhabbaṃ phusitvā - pe - manasā dhammaṃ viññāya na nimittaggāhī - pe - manindriye saṃvaraṃ āpajjatī"ti (ma. ni. 1.22, 411; dī. ni. 1.213; a. ni. 4.198) vuttaṃ, idaṃ indriyasaṃvarasīlaṃ.
rows.english: On hearing a sound with the ear … On smelling an odour with the nose … On tasting a flavour with the tongue … On touching a tangible object with the body … On cognizing a mental object with the mind, he apprehends neither the signs nor the particulars through which, if he left the mind faculty unguarded, evil and unprofitable states of covetousness and grief might invade him; he enters upon the way of its restraint, he guards the mind faculty, undertakes the restraint of the mind faculty (M I 180), is virtue of restraint of the sense faculties.

rows.pali: Yā pana ājīvahetupaññattānaṃ channaṃ sikkhāpadānaṃ vītikkamassa, "kuhanā lapanā nemittikatā nippesikatā lābhena lābhaṃ nijigīsanatā"ti evamādīnañca pāpadhammānaṃ vasena pavattā micchājīvā virati, idaṃ ājīvapārisuddhisīlaṃ.
rows.english: (c) Abstinence from such wrong livelihood as entails transgression of the six training precepts announced with respect to livelihood and entails the evil states beginning with “Scheming, talking, hinting, belittling, pursuing gain with gain” (M II 75) is virtue of livelihood purification.

rows.pali: "Paṭisaṅkhā yoniso cīvaraṃ paṭisevati, yāvadeva sītassa paṭighātāyā"ti (ma. ni. 1.23; a. ni. 6.58) ādinā nayena vutto paṭisaṅkhānaparisuddho catupaccayaparibhogo paccayasannissitasīlaṃ nāma.
rows.english: (d) Use of the four requisites that is purified by the reflection stated in the way beginning, “Reflecting wisely, he uses the robe only for protection from cold” (M I 10) is called virtue concerning requisites.

rows.pali: 14.Tatrāyaṃ ādito paṭṭhāya anupubbapadavaṇṇanāya saddhiṃ vinicchayakathā.
rows.english: 43.Here is an explanatory exposition together with a word commentary starting from the beginning.

rows.pali: Idhāti imasmiṃ sāsane.
rows.english: (a) Here: in this dispensation.

rows.pali: Bhikkhūti saṃsāre bhayaṃ ikkhaṇatāya vā bhinnapaṭadharāditāya vā evaṃ laddhavohāro saddhāpabbajito kulaputto.
rows.english: A bhikkhu: a clansman who has gone forth out of faith and is so styled because he sees fear in the round of rebirths (saṃsāre bhayaṃ ikkhanatā) or because he wears cloth garments that are torn and pieced together, and so on.

rows.pali: Pātimokkhasaṃvarasaṃvutoti ettha pātimokkhanti sikkhāpadasīlaṃ.
rows.english: Restrained with the Pātimokkha restraint: here “Pātimokkha” (Rule of the Community)11 is the virtue of the training precepts;

rows.pali: Tañhi yo naṃ pāti rakkhati, taṃ mokkheti mocayati āpāyikādīhi dukkhehi, tasmā pātimokkhanti vuccati.
rows.english: for it frees (mokkheti) him who protects (pāti) it, guards it, it sets him free (mocayati) from the pains of the states of loss, etc., that is why it is called Pātimokkha.

rows.pali: Saṃvaraṇaṃ saṃvaro, kāyikavācasikassa avītikkamassetaṃ nāmaṃ.
rows.english: “Restraint” is restraining; this is a term for bodily and verbal non-transgression.

rows.pali: Pātimokkhameva saṃvaro pātimokkhasaṃvaro.
rows.english: The Pātimokkha itself as restraint is “Pātimokkha restraint.”

rows.pali: Tena pātimokkhasaṃvarena saṃvuto pātimokkhasaṃvarasaṃvuto, upagato samannāgatoti attho.
rows.english: “Restrained with the Pātimokkha restraint” is restrained by means of the restraint consisting in that Pātimokkha; he has it, possesses it, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Viharatīti iriyati.
rows.english: Dwells: bears himself in one of the postures.

rows.pali: Ācāragocarasampannotiādīnamattho pāḷiyaṃ āgatanayeneva veditabbo.
rows.english: 44. The meaning of possessed of [the proper] conduct and resort, etc., should be understood in the way in which it is given in the text.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ –
rows.english: For this is said:

rows.pali: Tattha katamo anācāro?
rows.english: Herein, what is improper conduct?

rows.pali: Kāyiko vītikkamo vācasiko vītikkamo kāyikavācasiko vītikkamo, ayaṃ vuccati anācāro.
rows.english: Bodily transgression, verbal transgression, bodily and verbal transgression—this is called improper conduct.

rows.pali: Sabbampi dussīlyaṃ anācāro.
rows.english: Also all unvirtuousness is improper conduct.

rows.pali: Idhekacco veḷudānena vā pattadānena vā pupphaphalasinānadantakaṭṭhadānena vā cāṭukamyatāya vā muggasūpyatāya vā pāribhaṭyatāya vā jaṅghapesanikena vā aññataraññatarena vā buddhapaṭikuṭṭhena micchāājīvena jīvikaṃ kappeti, ayaṃ vuccati anācāro.
rows.english: Here someone makes a livelihood by gifts of bamboos, or by gifts of leaves, or by gifts of flowers, fruits, bathing powder, and tooth sticks, or by flattery, or by bean-soupery, or by fondling, or by going on errands on foot, or by one or other of the sorts of wrong livelihood condemned by the Buddhas—this is called improper conduct.

rows.pali: Tattha katamo ācāro?
rows.english: Herein, what is [proper] conduct?

rows.pali: Kāyiko avītikkamo vācasiko avītikkamo kāyikavācasiko avītikkamo, ayaṃ vuccati ācāro.
rows.english: Bodily non-transgression, verbal non-transgression, bodily and verbal non-transgression— this is called [proper] conduct.

rows.pali: Sabbopi sīlasaṃvaro ācāro.
rows.english: Also all restraint through virtue is [proper] conduct.

rows.pali: Idhekacco na veḷudānena vā na pattana pupphana phalana sinānana dantakaṭṭhadānena vā na cāṭukamyatāya vā na muggasūpyatāya vā na pāribhaṭyatāya vā na jaṅghapesanikena vā na aññataraññatarena vā buddhapaṭikuṭṭhena micchāājīvena jīvikaṃ kappeti, ayaṃ vuccati ācāro.
rows.english: Here someone “does not make a livelihood by gifts of bamboos, or by gifts of leaves, or by gifts of flowers, fruits, bathing powder, and tooth sticks, or by flattery, or by bean-soupery, or by fondling, or by going on errands on foot, or by one or other of the sorts of wrong livelihood condemned by the Buddhas—this is called [proper] conduct.”

rows.pali: Gocaroti atthi gocaro atthi agocaro.
rows.english: 45.“[Proper] resort: there is [proper] resort and improper resort.

rows.pali: Tattha katamo agocaro?
rows.english: Herein, what is improper resort?

rows.pali: Idhekacco vesiyāgocaro vā hoti vidhavā, thullakumārikā, paṇḍaka, bhikkhunī, pānāgāragocaro vā hoti, saṃsaṭṭho viharati rājūhi rājamahāmattehi titthiyehi titthiyasāvakehi ananulomikena saṃsaggena, yāni vā pana tāni kulāni assaddhāni appasannāni anopānabhūtāni akkosakaparibhāsakāni anatthakāmāni ahitakāmāni aphāsukakāmāni ayogakkhemakāmāni bhikkhūnaṃ bhikkhunīnaṃ upāsakānaṃ upāsikānaṃ, tathārūpāni kulāni sevati bhajati payirupāsati, ayaṃ vuccati agocaro.
rows.english: Here someone has prostitutes as resort, or he has widows, old maids, eunuchs, bhikkhunīs, or taverns as resort; or he dwells associated with kings, kings’ ministers, sectarians, sectarians’ disciples, in unbecoming association with laymen; or he cultivates, frequents, honours, such families as are faithless, untrusting, abusive and rude, who wish harm, wish ill, wish woe, wish no surcease of bondage, for bhikkhus and bhikkhunīs, for male and female devotees —this is called improper resort.

rows.pali: Tattha katamo gocaro?
rows.english: Herein, what is [proper] resort?

rows.pali: Idhekacco na vesiyāgocaro vā hoti - pe - na pānāgāragocaro vā hoti, asaṃsaṭṭho viharati rājūhi - pe - titthiyasāvakehi ananulomikena saṃsaggena, yāni vā pana tāni kulāni saddhāni pasannāni opānabhūtāni kāsāvapajjotāni isivātapaṭivātāni atthakāmāni - pe - yogakkhemakāmāni bhikkhūnaṃ - pe - upāsikānaṃ, tathārūpāni kulāni sevati bhajati payirupāsati, ayaṃ vuccati gocaro.
rows.english: Here someone does not have prostitutes as resort … or taverns as resort; he does not dwell associated with kings … sectarians’ disciples, in unbecoming association with laymen; he cultivates, frequents, honours, such families as are faithful and trusting, who are a solace, where the yellow cloth glows, where the breeze of sages blows, who wish good, wish well, wish joy, wish surcease of bondage, for bhikkhus and bhikkhunīs, for male and female devotees—this is called [proper] resort.

rows.pali: Iti iminā ca ācārena iminā ca gocarena upeto hoti samupeto upagato samupagato upapanno sampanno samannāgato, tena vuccati "ācāragocarasampanno"ti (vibha. 511).
rows.english: Thus he is furnished with, fully furnished with, provided with, fully provided with, supplied with, possessed of, endowed with, this [proper] conduct and this [proper] resort. Hence it is said, ’Possessed of [the proper] conduct and resort’” (Vibh 246–47).

rows.pali: Api cettha imināpi nayena ācāragocarā veditabbā.
rows.english: 46.Furthermore, [proper] conduct and resort should also be understood here in the following way;

rows.pali: Duvidho hi anācāro kāyiko vācasiko ca.
rows.english: for improper conduct is twofold as bodily and verbal.

rows.pali: Tattha katamo kāyiko anācāro?
rows.english: Herein, what is bodily improper conduct?

rows.pali: Idhekacco saṅghagatopi acittīkārakato there bhikkhū ghaṭṭayantopi tiṭṭhati, ghaṭṭayantopi nisīdati, puratopi tiṭṭhati, puratopi nisīdati, uccepi āsane nisīdati, sasīsampi pārupitvā nisīdati, ṭhitakopi bhaṇati, bāhāvikkhepakopi bhaṇati, therānaṃ bhikkhūnaṃ anupāhanānaṃ caṅkamantānaṃ saupāhano caṅkamati, nīce caṅkame caṅkamantānaṃ ucce caṅkame caṅkamati, chamāya caṅkamantānaṃ caṅkame caṅkamati, there bhikkhū anupakhajjāpi tiṭṭhati, anupakhajjāpi nisīdati, navepi bhikkhū āsanena paṭibāhati, jantāgharepi there bhikkhū anāpucchā kaṭṭhaṃ pakkhipati, dvāraṃ pidahati, udakatitthepi there bhikkhū ghaṭṭayantopi otarati, puratopi otarati, ghaṭṭayantopi nhāyati, puratopi nhāyati, ghaṭṭayantopi uttarati, puratopi uttarati, antaragharaṃ pavisantopi there bhikkhū ghaṭṭayantopi gacchati, puratopi gacchati, vokkamma ca therānaṃ bhikkhūnaṃ purato purato gacchati, yānipi tāni honti kulānaṃ ovarakāni gūḷhāni ca paṭicchannāni ca yattha kulitthiyo kulakumāriyo nisīdanti, tatthapi sahasā pavisati, kumārakassapi sīsaṃ parāmasati, ayaṃ vuccati kāyiko anācāro.
rows.english: “Here someone acts disrespectfully before the Community, and he stands jostling elder bhikkhus, sits jostling them, stands in front of them, sits in front of them, sits on a high seat, sits with his head covered, talks standing up, talks waving his arms … walks with sandals while elder bhikkhus walk without sandals, walks on a high walk while they walk on a low walk, walks on a walk while they walk on the ground … stands pushing elder bhikkhus, sits pushing them, prevents new bhikkhus from getting a seat … and in the bath house … without asking elder bhikkhus he puts wood on [the stove] … bolts the door … and at the bathing place he enters the water jostling elder bhikkhus, enters it in front of them, bathes jostling them, bathes in front of them, comes out jostling them, comes out in front of them … and entering inside a house he goes jostling elder bhikkhus, goes in front of them, pushing forward he goes in front of them … and where families have inner private screened rooms in which the women of the family … the girls of the family, sit, there he enters abruptly, and he strokes a child’s head” (Nidd I 228–29). This is called bodily improper conduct.

rows.pali: Tattha katamo vācasiko anācāro?
rows.english: 47.Herein, what is verbal improper conduct?

rows.pali: Idhekacco saṅghagatopi acittīkārakato there bhikkhū anāpucchā dhammaṃ bhaṇati.
rows.english: “Here someone acts disrespectfully before the Community. Without asking elder bhikkhus he talks on the Dhamma,

rows.pali: Pañhaṃ vissajjeti, pātimokkhaṃ uddisati, ṭhitakopi bhaṇati, bāhāvikkhepakopi bhaṇati, antaragharaṃ paviṭṭhopi itthiṃ vā kumāriṃ vā evamāha – "itthannāme itthaṃgotte kiṃ atthi, yāgu atthi, bhattaṃ atthi, khādanīyaṃ atthi, kiṃ pivissāma, kiṃ khādissāma, kiṃ bhuñjissāma.
rows.english: answers questions, recites the Pātimokkha, talks standing up, talks waving his arms … having entered inside a house, he speaks to a woman or a girl thus: ‘You, so- and-so of such-and-such a clan, what is there? Is there rice gruel? Is there cooked rice? Is there any hard food to eat? What shall we drink? What hard food shall we eat? What soft food shall we eat?

rows.pali: Kiṃ vā me dassathā"ti vippalapati, ayaṃ vuccati vācasiko anācāro (mahāni. 87).
rows.english: Or what will you give me?’ —he chatters like this” (Nidd I 230). This is called verbal improper conduct.

rows.pali: Paṭipakkhavasena panassa ācāro veditabbo.
rows.english: 48. Proper conduct should be understood in the opposite sense to that.

rows.pali: Apica bhikkhu sagāravo sappatisso hirottappasampanno sunivattho supāruto pāsādikena abhikkantena paṭikkantena ālokitena vilokitena samiñjitena pasāritena okkhittacakkhu iriyāpathasampanno indriyesu guttadvāro bhojane mattaññū jāgariyamanuyutto satisampajaññena samannāgato appiccho santuṭṭho āraddhavīriyo ābhisamācārikesu sakkaccakārī garucittīkārabahulo viharati, ayaṃ vuccati ācāro.
rows.english: Furthermore, a bhikkhu is respectful, deferential, possessed of conscience and shame, wears his inner robe properly, wears his upper robe properly, his manner inspires confidence whether in moving forwards or backwards, looking ahead or aside, bending or stretching, his eyes are downcast, he has (a good) deportment, he guards the doors of his sense faculties, knows the right measure in eating, is devoted to wakefulness, possesses mindfulness and full awareness, wants little, is contented, is strenuous, is a careful observer of good behaviour, and treats the teachers with great respect. This is called (proper) conduct.

rows.pali: Evaṃ tāva ācāro veditabbo.
rows.english: This firstly is how (proper) conduct should be understood.

rows.pali: Gocaro pana tividho upanissayagocaro ārakkhagocaro upanibandhagocaroti.
rows.english: 49.(Proper) resort is of three kinds: (proper) resort as support, (proper) resort as guarding, and (proper) resort as anchoring.

rows.pali: Tattha katamo upanissayagocaro?
rows.english: Herein, what is (proper) resort as support?

rows.pali: Dasakathāvatthuguṇasamannāgato kalyāṇamitto, yaṃ nissāya assutaṃ suṇāti, sutaṃ pariyodapeti, kaṅkhaṃ vitarati, diṭṭhiṃ ujuṃ karoti, cittaṃ pasādeti.
rows.english: A good friend who exhibits the instances of talk,12 in whose presence one hears what has not been heard, corrects what has been heard, gets rid of doubt, rectifies one’s view, and gains confidence;

rows.pali: Yassa vā pana anusikkhamāno saddhāya vaḍḍhati, sīlena, sutena, cāgena, paññāya vaḍḍhati, ayaṃ vuccati upanissayagocaro.
rows.english: or by training under whom one grows in faith, virtue, learning, generosity and understanding—this is called (proper) resort as support.

rows.pali: Katamo ārakkhagocaro?
rows.english: 50.What is (proper) resort as guarding?

rows.pali: Idha bhikkhu antaragharaṃ paviṭṭho vīthiṃ paṭipanno okkhittacakkhu yugamattadassāvī susaṃvuto gacchati, na hatthiṃ olokento, na assaṃ, na rathaṃ, na pattiṃ, na itthiṃ, na purisaṃ olokento, na uddhaṃ ullokento, na adho olokento, na disāvidisaṃ pekkhamāno gacchati, ayaṃ vuccati ārakkhagocaro.
rows.english: Here “A bhikkhu, having entered inside a house, having gone into a street, goes with downcast eyes, seeing the length of a plough yoke, restrained, not looking at an elephant, not looking at a horse, a carriage, a pedestrian, a woman, a man, not looking up, not looking down, not staring this way and that” (Nidd I 474). This is called (proper) resort as guarding.

rows.pali: Katamo upanibandhagocaro?
rows.english: 51.What is (proper) resort as anchoring?

rows.pali: Cattāro satipaṭṭhānā yattha cittaṃ upanibandhati.
rows.english: It is the four foundations of mindfulness on which the mind is anchored;

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā – "ko ca, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno gocaro sako pettiko visayo?
rows.english: for this is said by the Blessed One: “Bhikkhus, what is a bhikkhu’s resort, his own native place?

rows.pali: Yadidaṃ cattāro satipaṭṭhānā"ti (saṃ. ni. 5.372), ayaṃ vuccati upanibandhagocaro.
rows.english: It is these four foundations of mindfulness” (S V 148). This is called (proper) resort as anchoring.

rows.pali: Iti iminā ca ācārena iminā ca gocarena upeto - pe - samannāgato.
rows.english: Being thus furnished with … endowed with, this (proper) conduct and this (proper) resort,

rows.pali: Tenapi vuccati ācāragocarasampannoti.
rows.english: he is also on that account called “one possessed of (proper) conduct and resort.”

rows.pali: Aṇumattesu vajjesu bhayadassāvīti aṇuppamāṇesu asañcicca āpannasekhiyaakusalacittuppādādibhedesu vajjesu bhayadassanasīlo.
rows.english: 52.Seeing fear in the slightest fault (§42): one who has the habit (sīla) of seeing fear in faults of the minutest measure, of such kinds as unintentional contravening of a minor training rule of the Pātimokkha, or the arising of unprofitable thoughts.

rows.pali: Samādāya sikkhati sikkhāpadesūti yaṃkiñci sikkhāpadesu sikkhitabbaṃ, taṃ sabbaṃ sammā ādāya sikkhati.
rows.english: He trains himself by undertaking (samādāya) the precepts of training: whatever there is among the precepts of training to be trained in, in all that he trains by taking it up rightly (sammā ādāya).

rows.pali: Ettha ca "pātimokkhasaṃvarasaṃvuto"ti ettāvatā ca puggalādhiṭṭhānāya desanāya pātimokkhasaṃvarasīlaṃ dassitaṃ.
rows.english: And here, as far as the words, “one restrained by the Pātimokkha restraint,” virtue of Pātimokkha restraint is shown by discourse in terms of persons.13

rows.pali: "Ācāragocarasampanno"tiādi pana sabbaṃ yathāpaṭipannassa taṃ sīlaṃ sampajjati, taṃ paṭipattiṃ dassetuṃ vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: But all that beginning with the words, “possessed of [proper] conduct and resort” should be understood as said in order to show the way of practice that perfects that virtue in him who so practices it.

rows.pali: 15.Yaṃ panetaṃ tadanantaraṃ "so cakkhunā rūpaṃ disvā"tiādinā nayena dassitaṃ indriyasaṃvarasīlaṃ, tattha soti pātimokkhasaṃvarasīle ṭhito bhikkhu.
rows.english: 53.(b) Now, as regards the virtue of restraint of faculties shown next to that in the way beginning, “on seeing a visible object with the eye,” herein he is a bhikkhu established in the virtue of Pātimokkha restraint.

rows.pali: Cakkhunā rūpaṃ disvāti kāraṇavasena cakkhūti laddhavohārena rūpadassanasamatthena cakkhuviññāṇena rūpaṃ disvā.
rows.english: On seeing a visible object with the eye: on seeing a visible object with the eye-consciousness that is capable of seeing visible objects and has borrowed the name “eye” from its instrument.

rows.pali: Porāṇā panāhu "cakkhu rūpaṃ na passati, acittakattā, cittaṃ na passati, acakkhukattā, dvārārammaṇasaṅghaṭṭe pana cakkhupasādavatthukena cittena passati.
rows.english: But the Ancients (porāṇā) said: “The eye does not see a visible object because it has no mind. The mind does not see because it has no eyes. But when there is the impingement of door and object he sees by means of the consciousness that has eye-sensitivity as its physical basis.

rows.pali: Īdisī panesā 'dhanunā vijjhatī'tiādīsu viya sasambhārakathā nāma hoti, tasmā cakkhuviññāṇena rūpaṃ disvāti ayamevettha attho"ti.
rows.english: Now, (an idiom) such as this is called an ‘accessory locution’ (sasambhārakathā), like ‘He shot him with his bow,’ and so on.So the meaning here is this: ‘On seeing a visible object with eye-consciousness.’”14

rows.pali: Na nimittaggāhīti itthipurisanimittaṃ vā subhanimittādikaṃ vā kilesavatthubhūtaṃ nimittaṃ na gaṇhāti, diṭṭhamatteyeva saṇṭhāti.
rows.english: 54.Apprehends neither the signs: he does not apprehend the sign of woman or man, or any sign that is a basis for defilement such as the sign of beauty, etc.; he stops at what is merely seen.

rows.pali: Nānubyañjanaggāhīti kilesānaṃ anuanubyañjanato pākaṭabhāvakaraṇato anubyañjananti laddhavohāraṃ hatthapādasitahasitakathitavilokitādibhedaṃ ākāraṃ na gaṇhāti, yaṃ tattha bhūtaṃ, tadeva gaṇhāti, cetiyapabbatavāsī mahātissatthero viya.
rows.english: Nor the particulars: he does not apprehend any aspect classed as hand, foot, smile, laughter, talk, looking ahead, looking aside, etc., which has acquired the name “particular” (anubyañjana) because of its particularizing (anu anu byañjanato) defilements, because of its making them manifest themselves. He only apprehends what is really there. Like the Elder Mahā Tissa who dwelt at Cetiyapabbata.

rows.pali: Theraṃ kira cetiyapabbatā anurādhapuraṃ piṇḍacāratthāya āgacchantaṃ aññatarā kulasuṇhā sāmikena saddhiṃ bhaṇḍitvā sumaṇḍitapasādhitā devakaññā viya kālasseva anurādhapurato nikkhamitvā ñātigharaṃ gacchantī antarāmagge disvā vipallatthacittā mahāhasitaṃ hasi.
rows.english: 55.It seems that as the elder was on his way from Cetiyapabbata to Anurādhapura for alms, a certain daughterinlaw of a clan, who had quarrelled with her husband and had set out early from Anurādhapura all dressed up and tricked out like a celestial nymph to go to her relatives’ home, saw him on the road, and being low- minded, [21] she laughed a loud laugh.

rows.pali: Thero kimetanti olokento tassā dantaṭṭhike asubhasaññaṃ paṭilabhitvā arahattaṃ pāpuṇi.
rows.english: [Wondering] “What is that?” the elder looked up and finding in the bones of her teeth the perception of foulness (ugliness), he reached Arahantship.15

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ –
rows.english: Hence it was said:

rows.pali: Sāmikopi kho panassā anumaggaṃ gacchanto theraṃ disvā "kiñci, bhante, itthiṃ passathā"ti pucchi.
rows.english: But her husband, who was going after her, saw the elder and asked, “Venerable sir, did you by any chance see a woman?

rows.pali: Taṃ thero āha –
rows.english: ” The elder told him:

rows.pali: Yatvādhikaraṇamenantiādimhi yaṃkāraṇā yassa cakkhundriyāsaṃvarassa hetu etaṃ puggalaṃ satikavāṭena cakkhundriyaṃ asaṃvutaṃ apihitacakkhudvāraṃ hutvā viharantaṃ ete abhijjhādayo dhammā anvāssaveyyuṃ anubandheyyuṃ.
rows.english: 56.As to the words through which, etc., the meaning is: by reason of which, because of which non-restraint of the eye faculty, if he, if that person, left the eye faculty unguarded, remained with the eye door unclosed by the door-panel of mindfulness, these states of covetousness, etc., might invade, might pursue, might threaten, him.

rows.pali: Tassa saṃvarāya paṭipajjatīti tassa cakkhundriyassa satikavāṭena pidahanatthāya paṭipajjati.
rows.english: He enters upon the way of its restraint: he enters upon the way of closing that eye faculty by the door-panel of mindfulness.

rows.pali: Evaṃ paṭipajjantoyeva ca rakkhati cakkhundriyaṃ, cakkhundriye saṃvaraṃ āpajjatītipi vuccati.
rows.english: It is the same one of whom it is said he guards the eye faculty, undertakes the restraint of the eye faculty.

rows.pali: Tattha kiñcāpi cakkhundriye saṃvaro vā asaṃvaro vā natthi.
rows.english: 57.Herein, there is neither restraint nor non-restraint in the actual eye faculty,

rows.pali: Na hi cakkhupasādaṃ nissāya sati vā muṭṭhasaccaṃ vā uppajjati.
rows.english: since neither mindfulness nor forgetfulness arises in dependence on eye-sensitivity.

rows.pali: Apica yadā rūpārammaṇaṃ cakkhussa āpāthaṃ āgacchati, tadā bhavaṅge dvikkhattuṃ uppajjitvā niruddhe kiriyamanodhātu āvajjanakiccaṃ sādhayamānā uppajjitvā nirujjhati.
rows.english: On the contrary when a visible datum as object comes into the eye’s focus, then, after the life-continuum has arisen twice and ceased, the functional mind-element accomplishing the function of adverting arises and ceases.

rows.pali: Tato cakkhuviññāṇaṃ dassanakiccaṃ.
rows.english: After that, eye- consciousness with the function of seeing;

rows.pali: Tato vipākamanodhātu sampaṭicchanakiccaṃ.
rows.english: after that, resultant mind-element with the function of receiving;

rows.pali: Tato vipākāhetukamanoviññāṇadhātu santīraṇakiccaṃ.
rows.english: after that, resultant root-causeless mind-consciousness- element with the function of investigating;

rows.pali: Tato kiriyāhetukamanoviññāṇadhātu voṭṭhabbanakiccaṃ sādhayamānā uppajjitvā nirujjhati, tadanantaraṃ javanaṃ javati.
rows.english: after that, functional root-causeless mind-consciousness-element accomplishing the function of determining arises and ceases. Next to that, impulsion impels.16

rows.pali: Tatrāpi neva bhavaṅgasamaye, na āvajjanādīnaṃ aññatarasamaye saṃvaro vā asaṃvaro vā atthi.
rows.english: Herein, there is neither restraint nor non- restraint on the occasion of the life-continuum, or on any of the occasions beginning with adverting.

rows.pali: Javanakkhaṇe pana sace dussīlyaṃ vā muṭṭhasaccaṃ vā aññāṇaṃ vā akkhanti vā kosajjaṃ vā uppajjati, asaṃvaro hoti.
rows.english: But there is non-restraint if unvirtuousness or forgetfulness or unknowing or impatience or idleness arises at the moment of impulsion.

rows.pali: Evaṃ honto pana so cakkhundriye asaṃvaroti vuccati.
rows.english: When this happens, it is called “non-restraint in the eye faculty.” [22]

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: 58.Why is that?

rows.pali: Yasmā tasmiṃ sati dvārampi aguttaṃ hoti, bhavaṅgampi āvajjanādīnipi vīthicittāni.
rows.english: Because when this happens, the door is not guarded, nor are the life-continuum and the consciousnesses of the cognitive series.

rows.pali: Yathā kiṃ?
rows.english: Like what?

rows.pali: Yathā nagare catūsu dvāresu asaṃvutesu kiñcāpi antogharadvārakoṭṭhakagabbhādayo susaṃvutā honti, tathāpi antonagare sabbaṃ bhaṇḍaṃ arakkhitaṃ agopitameva hoti.
rows.english: Just as, when a city’s four gates are not secured, although inside the city house doors, storehouses, rooms, etc., are secured, yet all property inside the city is unguarded and unprotected

rows.pali: Nagaradvārena hi pavisitvā corā yadicchanti, taṃ kareyyuṃ, evameva javane dussīlyādīsu uppannesu tasmiṃ asaṃvare sati dvārampi aguttaṃ hoti, bhavaṅgampi āvajjanādīnipi vīthicittāni.
rows.english: since robbers coming in by the city gates can do as they please, so too, when unvirtuousness, etc., arise in impulsion in which there is no restraint, then the door too is unguarded, and so also are the life-continuum and the consciousnesses of the cognitive series beginning with adverting.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ pana sīlādīsu uppannesu dvārampi guttaṃ hoti, bhavaṅgampi āvajjanādīnipi vīthicittāni.
rows.english: But when virtue, etc., has arisen in it, then the door too is guarded and so also are the life-continuum and the consciousnesses of the cognitive series beginning with adverting.

rows.pali: Yathā kiṃ?
rows.english: Like what?

rows.pali: Yathā nagaradvāresu saṃvutesu kiñcāpi antogharādayo asaṃvutā honti, tathāpi antonagare sabbaṃ bhaṇḍaṃ surakkhitaṃ sugopitameva hoti.
rows.english: Just as, when the city gates are secured, although inside the city the houses, etc., are not secured, yet all property inside the city is well guarded, well protected,

rows.pali: Nagaradvāresu hi pihitesu corānaṃ paveso natthi, evameva javane sīlādīsu uppannesu dvārampi guttaṃ hoti, bhavaṅgampi āvajjanādīnipi vīthicittāni.
rows.english: since when the city gates are shut there is no ingress for robbers, so too, when virtue, etc., have arisen in impulsion, the door too is guarded and so also are the life-continuum and the consciousnesses of the cognitive series beginning with adverting.

rows.pali: Tasmā javanakkhaṇe uppajjamānopi cakkhundriye saṃvaroti vutto.
rows.english: Thus although it actually arises at the moment of impulsion, it is nevertheless called “restraint in the eye faculty.”

rows.pali: Sotena saddaṃ sutvātiādīsupi eseva nayo.
rows.english: 59.So also as regards the phrases on hearing a sound with the ear and so on.

rows.pali: Evamidaṃ saṅkhepato rūpādīsu kilesānubandhanimittādiggāhaparivajjanalakkhaṇaṃ indriyasaṃvarasīlanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: So it is this virtue, which in brief has the characteristic of avoiding apprehension of signs entailing defilement with respect to visible objects, etc.., that should be understood as virtue of restraint of faculties.

rows.pali: 16.Idāni indriyasaṃvarasīlānantaraṃ vutte ājīvapārisuddhisīle ājīvahetu paññattānaṃ channaṃ sikkhāpadānanti yāni tāni "ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā pāpiccho icchāpakato asantaṃ abhūtaṃ uttarimanussadhammaṃ ullapati, āpatti pārājikassa.
rows.english: 60.(c) Now, as regards the virtue of livelihood purification mentioned above next to the virtue of restraint of the faculties (§42), the words of the six precepts announced on account of livelihood mean, of the following six training precepts announced thus: “With livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes, lays claim to a higher than human state that is non-existent, not a fact,” the contravention of which is defeat (expulsion from the Order);

rows.pali: Ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā sañcarittaṃ samāpajjati, āpatti saṅghādisesassa.
rows.english: “with livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, he acts as go-between,” the contravention of which is an offence entailing a meeting of the Order;

rows.pali: Ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā 'yo te vihāre vasati so bhikkhu arahā'ti bhaṇati, paṭivijānantassa āpatti thullaccayassa.
rows.english: “with livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, he says, ‘A bhikkhu who lives in your monastery is an Arahant,’” the contravention of which is a serious offence in one who is aware of it;

rows.pali: Ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā bhikkhu paṇītabhojanāni agilāno attano atthāya viññāpetvā bhuñjati, āpatti pācittiyassa.
rows.english: “with livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, a bhikkhu who is not sick eats superior food that he has ordered for his own use,” the contravention of which is an offence requiring expiation:

rows.pali: Ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā bhikkhunī paṇītabhojanāni agilānā attano atthāya viññāpetvā bhuñjati, āpatti pāṭidesanīyassa.
rows.english: “With livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, a bhikkhunī who is not sick eats superior food that she has ordered for her own use,” the contravention of which is an offence requiring confession;

rows.pali: Ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā sūpaṃ vā odanaṃ vā agilāno attano atthāya viññāpetvā bhuñjati, āpatti dukkaṭassā"ti (pari. 287) evaṃ paññattāni cha sikkhāpadāni, imesaṃ channaṃ sikkhāpadānaṃ.
rows.english: “with livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, one who is not sick eats curry or boiled rice that he has ordered for his own use,” the contravention of which is an offence of wrongdoing (Vin V 146). Of these six precepts.17

rows.pali: Kuhanātiādīsu ayaṃ pāḷi, "tattha katamā kuhanā?
rows.english: 61.As regards scheming, etc. (§42), this is the text: “Herein, what is scheming?

rows.pali: Lābhasakkārasilokasannissitassa pāpicchassa icchāpakatassa yā paccayapaṭisevanasaṅkhātena vā sāmantajappitena vā iriyāpathassa vā aṭṭhapanā ṭhapanā saṇṭhapanā bhākuṭikā bhākuṭiyaṃ kuhanā kuhāyanā kuhitattaṃ, ayaṃ vuccati kuhanā.
rows.english: It is the grimacing, grimacery, scheming, schemery, schemedness,18 by what is called rejection of requisites or by indirect talk, or it is the disposing, posing, composing, of the deportment on the part of one bent on gain, honour and renown, of one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes—this is called scheming.

rows.pali: "Tattha katamā lapanā?
rows.english: 62.”Herein, what is talking?

rows.pali: Lābhasakkārasilokasannissitassa pāpicchassa icchāpakatassa yā paresaṃ ālapanā lapanā sallapanā ullapanā samullapanā unnahanā samunnahanā ukkācanā samukkācanā anuppiyabhāṇitā cāṭukamyatā muggasūpyatā pāribhaṭyatā, ayaṃ vuccati lapanā.
rows.english: Talking at others, talking, talking round, talking up, continual talking up, persuading, continual persuading, suggesting, continual suggesting, ingratiating chatter, flattery, bean-soupery, fondling, on the part of one bent on gain, honour and renown, of one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes—this is called talking.

rows.pali: "Tattha katamā nemittikatā?
rows.english: 63.”Herein, what is hinting?

rows.pali: Lābhasakkārasilokasannissitassa pāpicchassa icchāpakatassa yaṃ paresaṃ nimittaṃ nimittakammaṃ obhāso obhāsakammaṃ sāmantajappā parikathā, ayaṃ vuccati nemittikatā.
rows.english: A sign to others, giving a sign, indication, giving indication, indirect talk, roundabout talk, on the part of one bent on gain, honour and renown, of one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes—this is called hinting.

rows.pali: "Tattha katamā nippesikatā?
rows.english: 64. ”Herein, what is belittling?

rows.pali: Lābhasakkārasilokasannissitassa pāpicchassa icchāpakatassa yā paresaṃ akkosanā vambhanā garahanā ukkhepanā samukkhepanā khipanā saṃkhipanā pāpanā sampāpanā avaṇṇahārikā parapiṭṭhimaṃsikatā, ayaṃ vuccati nippesikatā.
rows.english: Abusing of others, disparaging, reproaching, snubbing, continual snubbing, ridicule, continual ridicule, denigration, continual denigration, tale-bearing, backbiting, on the part of one bent on gain, honour and renown, of one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes—this is called belittling.

rows.pali: "Tattha katamā lābhena lābhaṃ nijigīsanatā?
rows.english: 65.”Herein, what is pursuing gain with gain?

rows.pali: Lābhasakkārasilokasannissito pāpiccho icchāpakato ito laddhaṃ āmisaṃ amutra harati, amutra vā laddhaṃ āmisaṃ idha āharati.
rows.english: Seeking, seeking for, seeking out, going in search of, searching for, searching out material goods by means of material goods, such as carrying there goods that have been got from here,

rows.pali: Yā evarūpā āmisena āmisassa eṭṭhi gaveṭṭhi pariyeṭṭhi esanā gavesanā pariyesanā, ayaṃ vuccati lābhena lābhaṃ nijigīsanatā"ti (vibha. 862-865).
rows.english: or carrying here goods that have been got from there, by one bent on gain, honour and renown, by one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes—this is called pursuing gain with gain.”19 (Vibh 352–53)

rows.pali: 17.Imissā pana pāḷiyā evamattho veditabbo.
rows.english: 66.The meaning of this text should be understood as follows:

rows.pali: Kuhananiddese tāva lābhasakkārasilokasannissitassāti lābhañca sakkārañca kittisaddañca sannissitassa, patthayantassāti attho.
rows.english: Firstly, as regards description of scheming: on the part of one bent on gain, honour and renown is on the part of one who is bent on gain, on honour, and on reputation; on the part of one who longs for them, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Pāpicchassāti asantaguṇadīpanakāmassa.
rows.english: Of one of evil wishes: of one who wants to show qualities that he has not got.

rows.pali: Icchāpakatassāti icchāya apakatassa, upaddutassāti attho.
rows.english: A prey to wishes:20 the meaning is, of one who is attacked by them.

rows.pali: Ito paraṃ yasmā paccayapaṭisevanasāmantajappanairiyāpathasannissitavasena mahāniddese tividhaṃ kuhanavatthu āgataṃ.
rows.english: And after this the passage beginning or by what is called rejection of requisites is given in order to show the three instances of scheming given in the Mahāniddesa as rejection of requisites, indirect talk, and that based on deportment.

rows.pali: Tasmā tividhampetaṃ dassetuṃ paccayapaṭisevanasaṅkhātena vāti evamādi āraddhaṃ.

Tattha cīvarādīhi nimantitassa tadatthikasseva sato pāpicchataṃ nissāya paṭikkhipanena, te ca gahapatike attani suppatiṭṭhitasaddhe ñatvā puna tesaṃ "aho ayyo appiccho na kiñci paṭiggaṇhituṃ icchati, suladdhaṃ vata no assa sace appamattakampi kiñci paṭiggaṇheyyā"ti nānāvidhehi upāyehi paṇītāni cīvarādīni upanentānaṃ tadanuggahakāmataṃyeva āvikatvā paṭiggahaṇena ca tato pabhuti api sakaṭabhārehi upanāmanahetubhūtaṃ vimhāpanaṃ paccayapaṭisevanasaṅkhātaṃ kuhanavatthūti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 67.Herein, [a bhikkhu] is invited to accept robes, etc., and, precisely because he wants them, he refuses them out of evil wishes. And then, since he knows that those householders believe in him implicitly when they think, “Oh, how few are our lord’s wishes! He will not accept a thing! ” and they put fine robes, etc., before him by various means, he then accepts, making a show that he wants to be compassionate towards them—it is this hypocrisy of his, which becomes the cause of their subsequently bringing them even by cartloads, that should be understood as the instance of scheming called rejection of requisites.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ mahāniddese –
rows.english: 68.For this is said in the Mahāniddesa:

rows.pali: Pāpicchasseva pana sato uttarimanussadhammādhigamaparidīpanavācāya tathā tathā vimhāpanaṃ sāmantajappanasaṅkhātaṃ kuhanavatthūti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 69.It is hypocrisy on the part of one of evil wishes, who gives it to be understood verbally in some way or other that he has attained a higher than human state, that should be understood as the instance of scheming called indirect talk,

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Pāpicchasseva pana sato sambhāvanādhippāyakatena iriyāpathena vimhāpanaṃ iriyāpathasannissitaṃ kuhanavatthūti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 70.It is hypocrisy on the part of one of evil wishes, which takes the form of deportment influenced by eagerness to be admired, that should be understood as the instance of scheming dependent on deportment,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "katamaṃ iriyāpathasaṅkhātaṃ kuhanavatthu.
rows.english: according as it is said: “What is the instance of scheming called deportment?

rows.pali: Idhekacco pāpiccho icchāpakato sambhāvanādhippāyo 'evaṃ maṃ jano sambhāvessatī'ti gamanaṃ saṇṭhapeti, ṭhānaṃ saṇṭhapeti, nisajjaṃ saṇṭhapeti, sayanaṃ saṇṭhapeti, paṇidhāya gacchati, paṇidhāya tiṭṭhati, paṇidhāya nisīdati, paṇidhāya seyyaṃ kappeti, samāhito viya gacchati, samāhito viya tiṭṭhati, nisīdati, seyyaṃ kappeti, āpāthakajjhāyī ca hoti, yā evarūpā iriyāpathassa aṭṭhapanā ṭhapanā saṇṭhapanā bhākuṭikā bhākuṭiyaṃ kuhanā kuhāyanā kuhitattaṃ, idaṃ vuccati iriyāpathasaṅkhātaṃ kuhanavatthū"ti (mahāni. 87).
rows.english: Here someone of evil wishes, a prey to wishes, eager to be admired, [thinking] ‘Thus people will admire me,’ composes his way of walking, composes his way of lying down; he walks studiedly, stands studiedly, sits studiedly, lies down studiedly; he walks as though concentrated, stands, sits, lies down as though concentrated; and he is one who meditates in public. Such disposing, posing, composing, of deportment, grimacing, grimacery, scheming, schemery, schemedness, is known as the instance of scheming called deportment” (Nidd I 225–26).

rows.pali: Tattha paccayapaṭisevanasaṅkhātenāti paccayapaṭisevananti evaṃ saṅkhātena paccayapaṭisevanena vā saṅkhātena.
rows.english: 71.Herein, the words by what is called rejection of requisites (§61) mean: by what is called thus “rejection of requisites”; or they mean: by means of the rejection of requisites that is so called.

rows.pali: Sāmantajappitenāti samīpabhaṇitena.
rows.english: By indirect talk means: by talking near to the subject.

rows.pali: Iriyāpathassa vāti catuiriyāpathassa.
rows.english: Of deportment means: of the four modes of deportment (postures).

rows.pali: Aṭṭhapanātiādi ṭhapanā, ādarena vā ṭhapanā.
rows.english: Disposing is initial posing, or careful posing.

rows.pali: Ṭhapanāti ṭhapanākāro.
rows.english: Posing is the manner of posing.

rows.pali: Saṇṭhapanāti abhisaṅkharaṇā, pāsādikabhāvakaraṇanti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Composing is prearranging; assuming a trust-inspiring attitude, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Bhākuṭikāti padhānapurimaṭṭhitabhāvadassanena bhākuṭikaraṇaṃ, mukhasaṅkocoti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Grimacing is making grimaces by showing great intenseness; facial contraction is what is meant.

rows.pali: Bhākuṭikaraṇaṃ sīlamassāti bhākuṭiko.
rows.english: One who has the habit of making grimaces is a grimacer.

rows.pali: Bhākuṭikassa bhāvo bhākuṭiyaṃ.
rows.english: The grimacer’s state is grimacery.

rows.pali: Kuhanāti vimhāpanā.
rows.english: Scheming is hypocrisy.

rows.pali: Kuhassa āyanā kuhāyanā.
rows.english: The way (āyanā) of a schemer (kuha) is schemery (kuhāyanā).

rows.pali: Kuhitassa bhāvo kuhitattanti.
rows.english: The state of what is schemed is schemedness.

rows.pali: Lapanāniddese ālapanāti vihāraṃ āgate manusse disvā "kimatthāya bhonto āgatā, kiṃ bhikkhū nimantituṃ, yadi evaṃ gacchatha re, ahaṃ pacchato pattaṃ gahetvā āgacchāmī"ti evaṃ āditova lapanā.
rows.english: 72.In the description of talking: talking at is talking thus on seeing people coming to the monastery, “What have you come for, good people? What, to invite bhikkhus? If it is that, then go along and I shall come later with [my bowl],” etc.;

rows.pali: Atha vā attānaṃ upanetvā "ahaṃ tisso, mayi rājā pasanno, mayi asuko ca asuko ca rājamahāmatto pasanno"ti evaṃ attupanāyikā lapanā ālapanā.
rows.english: or alternatively, talking at is talking by advertising oneself thus, “I am Tissa, the king trusts me, such and such king’s ministers trust me.”

rows.pali: Lapanāti puṭṭhassa sato vuttappakārameva lapanaṃ.
rows.english: Talking is the same kind of talking on being asked a question.

rows.pali: Sallapanāti gahapatikānaṃ ukkaṇṭhane bhītassa okāsaṃ datvā datvā suṭṭhu lapanā.
rows.english: Talking round is roundly talking by one who is afraid of householders’ displeasure because he has given occasion for it.

rows.pali: Ullapanāti mahākuṭumbiko mahānāviko mahādānapatīti evaṃ uddhaṃ katvā lapanā.
rows.english: Talking up is talking by extolling people thus, “He is a great land-owner, a great ship-owner, a great lord of giving.”

rows.pali: Samullapanāti sabbatobhāgena uddhaṃ katvā lapanā.
rows.english: Continual talking up is talking by extolling [people] in all ways.

rows.pali: Unnahanāti "upāsakā pubbe īdise kāle navadānaṃ detha, idāni kiṃ na dethā"ti evaṃ yāva "dassāma, bhante, okāsaṃ na labhāmā"tiādīni vadanti, tāva uddhaṃ uddhaṃ nahanā, veṭhanāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 73.Persuading is progressively involving23 [people] thus, “Lay followers, formerly you used to give first-fruit alms at such a time; why do you not do so now? ” until they say, “We shall give, venerable sir, we have had no opportunity,” etc.; entangling, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Atha vā ucchuhatthaṃ disvā "kuto ābhataṃ upāsakā"ti pucchati.
rows.english: Or alternatively, seeing someone with sugarcane in his hand, he asks, “Where are you coming from, lay follower?”

rows.pali: Ucchukhettato, bhanteti.
rows.english: —”From the sugarcane field, venerable sir”

rows.pali: Kiṃ tattha ucchu madhuranti.
rows.english: —”Is the sugarcane sweet there?”

rows.pali: Khāditvā, bhante, jānitabbanti.
rows.english: —”One can find out by eating, venerable sir”

rows.pali: "Na, upāsaka, bhikkhussa ucchuṃ dethā"ti vattuṃ vaṭṭatīti.
rows.english: —”It is not allowed, lay follower, for bhikkhus to say ‘Give [me some] sugarcane.’

rows.pali: Yā evarūpā nibbeṭhentassāpi veṭhanakathā, sā unnahanā.
rows.english: ” Such entangling talk from such an entangler is persuading.

rows.pali: Sabbatobhāgena punappunaṃ unnahanā samunnahanā.
rows.english: Persuading again and again in all ways is continual persuading.

rows.pali: Ukkācanāti "etaṃ kulaṃ maṃyeva jānāti.
rows.english: 74.Suggesting is insinuating by specifying thus, “That family alone understands me;
