rows.english: since neither mindfulness nor forgetfulness arises in dependence on eye-sensitivity.

rows.pali: Apica yadā rūpārammaṇaṃ cakkhussa āpāthaṃ āgacchati, tadā bhavaṅge dvikkhattuṃ uppajjitvā niruddhe kiriyamanodhātu āvajjanakiccaṃ sādhayamānā uppajjitvā nirujjhati.
rows.english: On the contrary when a visible datum as object comes into the eye’s focus, then, after the life-continuum has arisen twice and ceased, the functional mind-element accomplishing the function of adverting arises and ceases.

rows.pali: Tato cakkhuviññāṇaṃ dassanakiccaṃ.
rows.english: After that, eye- consciousness with the function of seeing;

rows.pali: Tato vipākamanodhātu sampaṭicchanakiccaṃ.
rows.english: after that, resultant mind-element with the function of receiving;

rows.pali: Tato vipākāhetukamanoviññāṇadhātu santīraṇakiccaṃ.
rows.english: after that, resultant root-causeless mind-consciousness- element with the function of investigating;

rows.pali: Tato kiriyāhetukamanoviññāṇadhātu voṭṭhabbanakiccaṃ sādhayamānā uppajjitvā nirujjhati, tadanantaraṃ javanaṃ javati.
rows.english: after that, functional root-causeless mind-consciousness-element accomplishing the function of determining arises and ceases. Next to that, impulsion impels.16

rows.pali: Tatrāpi neva bhavaṅgasamaye, na āvajjanādīnaṃ aññatarasamaye saṃvaro vā asaṃvaro vā atthi.
rows.english: Herein, there is neither restraint nor non- restraint on the occasion of the life-continuum, or on any of the occasions beginning with adverting.

rows.pali: Javanakkhaṇe pana sace dussīlyaṃ vā muṭṭhasaccaṃ vā aññāṇaṃ vā akkhanti vā kosajjaṃ vā uppajjati, asaṃvaro hoti.
rows.english: But there is non-restraint if unvirtuousness or forgetfulness or unknowing or impatience or idleness arises at the moment of impulsion.

rows.pali: Evaṃ honto pana so cakkhundriye asaṃvaroti vuccati.
rows.english: When this happens, it is called “non-restraint in the eye faculty.” [22]

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: 58.Why is that?

rows.pali: Yasmā tasmiṃ sati dvārampi aguttaṃ hoti, bhavaṅgampi āvajjanādīnipi vīthicittāni.
rows.english: Because when this happens, the door is not guarded, nor are the life-continuum and the consciousnesses of the cognitive series.

rows.pali: Yathā kiṃ?
rows.english: Like what?

rows.pali: Yathā nagare catūsu dvāresu asaṃvutesu kiñcāpi antogharadvārakoṭṭhakagabbhādayo susaṃvutā honti, tathāpi antonagare sabbaṃ bhaṇḍaṃ arakkhitaṃ agopitameva hoti.
rows.english: Just as, when a city’s four gates are not secured, although inside the city house doors, storehouses, rooms, etc., are secured, yet all property inside the city is unguarded and unprotected

rows.pali: Nagaradvārena hi pavisitvā corā yadicchanti, taṃ kareyyuṃ, evameva javane dussīlyādīsu uppannesu tasmiṃ asaṃvare sati dvārampi aguttaṃ hoti, bhavaṅgampi āvajjanādīnipi vīthicittāni.
rows.english: since robbers coming in by the city gates can do as they please, so too, when unvirtuousness, etc., arise in impulsion in which there is no restraint, then the door too is unguarded, and so also are the life-continuum and the consciousnesses of the cognitive series beginning with adverting.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ pana sīlādīsu uppannesu dvārampi guttaṃ hoti, bhavaṅgampi āvajjanādīnipi vīthicittāni.
rows.english: But when virtue, etc., has arisen in it, then the door too is guarded and so also are the life-continuum and the consciousnesses of the cognitive series beginning with adverting.

rows.pali: Yathā kiṃ?
rows.english: Like what?

rows.pali: Yathā nagaradvāresu saṃvutesu kiñcāpi antogharādayo asaṃvutā honti, tathāpi antonagare sabbaṃ bhaṇḍaṃ surakkhitaṃ sugopitameva hoti.
rows.english: Just as, when the city gates are secured, although inside the city the houses, etc., are not secured, yet all property inside the city is well guarded, well protected,

rows.pali: Nagaradvāresu hi pihitesu corānaṃ paveso natthi, evameva javane sīlādīsu uppannesu dvārampi guttaṃ hoti, bhavaṅgampi āvajjanādīnipi vīthicittāni.
rows.english: since when the city gates are shut there is no ingress for robbers, so too, when virtue, etc., have arisen in impulsion, the door too is guarded and so also are the life-continuum and the consciousnesses of the cognitive series beginning with adverting.

rows.pali: Tasmā javanakkhaṇe uppajjamānopi cakkhundriye saṃvaroti vutto.
rows.english: Thus although it actually arises at the moment of impulsion, it is nevertheless called “restraint in the eye faculty.”

rows.pali: Sotena saddaṃ sutvātiādīsupi eseva nayo.
rows.english: 59.So also as regards the phrases on hearing a sound with the ear and so on.

rows.pali: Evamidaṃ saṅkhepato rūpādīsu kilesānubandhanimittādiggāhaparivajjanalakkhaṇaṃ indriyasaṃvarasīlanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: So it is this virtue, which in brief has the characteristic of avoiding apprehension of signs entailing defilement with respect to visible objects, etc.., that should be understood as virtue of restraint of faculties.

rows.pali: 16.Idāni indriyasaṃvarasīlānantaraṃ vutte ājīvapārisuddhisīle ājīvahetu paññattānaṃ channaṃ sikkhāpadānanti yāni tāni "ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā pāpiccho icchāpakato asantaṃ abhūtaṃ uttarimanussadhammaṃ ullapati, āpatti pārājikassa.
rows.english: 60.(c) Now, as regards the virtue of livelihood purification mentioned above next to the virtue of restraint of the faculties (§42), the words of the six precepts announced on account of livelihood mean, of the following six training precepts announced thus: “With livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes, lays claim to a higher than human state that is non-existent, not a fact,” the contravention of which is defeat (expulsion from the Order);

rows.pali: Ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā sañcarittaṃ samāpajjati, āpatti saṅghādisesassa.
rows.english: “with livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, he acts as go-between,” the contravention of which is an offence entailing a meeting of the Order;

rows.pali: Ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā 'yo te vihāre vasati so bhikkhu arahā'ti bhaṇati, paṭivijānantassa āpatti thullaccayassa.
rows.english: “with livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, he says, ‘A bhikkhu who lives in your monastery is an Arahant,’” the contravention of which is a serious offence in one who is aware of it;

rows.pali: Ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā bhikkhu paṇītabhojanāni agilāno attano atthāya viññāpetvā bhuñjati, āpatti pācittiyassa.
rows.english: “with livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, a bhikkhu who is not sick eats superior food that he has ordered for his own use,” the contravention of which is an offence requiring expiation:

rows.pali: Ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā bhikkhunī paṇītabhojanāni agilānā attano atthāya viññāpetvā bhuñjati, āpatti pāṭidesanīyassa.
rows.english: “With livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, a bhikkhunī who is not sick eats superior food that she has ordered for her own use,” the contravention of which is an offence requiring confession;

rows.pali: Ājīvahetu ājīvakāraṇā sūpaṃ vā odanaṃ vā agilāno attano atthāya viññāpetvā bhuñjati, āpatti dukkaṭassā"ti (pari. 287) evaṃ paññattāni cha sikkhāpadāni, imesaṃ channaṃ sikkhāpadānaṃ.
rows.english: “with livelihood as cause, with livelihood as reason, one who is not sick eats curry or boiled rice that he has ordered for his own use,” the contravention of which is an offence of wrongdoing (Vin V 146). Of these six precepts.17

rows.pali: Kuhanātiādīsu ayaṃ pāḷi, "tattha katamā kuhanā?
rows.english: 61.As regards scheming, etc. (§42), this is the text: “Herein, what is scheming?

rows.pali: Lābhasakkārasilokasannissitassa pāpicchassa icchāpakatassa yā paccayapaṭisevanasaṅkhātena vā sāmantajappitena vā iriyāpathassa vā aṭṭhapanā ṭhapanā saṇṭhapanā bhākuṭikā bhākuṭiyaṃ kuhanā kuhāyanā kuhitattaṃ, ayaṃ vuccati kuhanā.
rows.english: It is the grimacing, grimacery, scheming, schemery, schemedness,18 by what is called rejection of requisites or by indirect talk, or it is the disposing, posing, composing, of the deportment on the part of one bent on gain, honour and renown, of one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes—this is called scheming.

rows.pali: "Tattha katamā lapanā?
rows.english: 62.”Herein, what is talking?

rows.pali: Lābhasakkārasilokasannissitassa pāpicchassa icchāpakatassa yā paresaṃ ālapanā lapanā sallapanā ullapanā samullapanā unnahanā samunnahanā ukkācanā samukkācanā anuppiyabhāṇitā cāṭukamyatā muggasūpyatā pāribhaṭyatā, ayaṃ vuccati lapanā.
rows.english: Talking at others, talking, talking round, talking up, continual talking up, persuading, continual persuading, suggesting, continual suggesting, ingratiating chatter, flattery, bean-soupery, fondling, on the part of one bent on gain, honour and renown, of one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes—this is called talking.

rows.pali: "Tattha katamā nemittikatā?
rows.english: 63.”Herein, what is hinting?

rows.pali: Lābhasakkārasilokasannissitassa pāpicchassa icchāpakatassa yaṃ paresaṃ nimittaṃ nimittakammaṃ obhāso obhāsakammaṃ sāmantajappā parikathā, ayaṃ vuccati nemittikatā.
rows.english: A sign to others, giving a sign, indication, giving indication, indirect talk, roundabout talk, on the part of one bent on gain, honour and renown, of one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes—this is called hinting.

rows.pali: "Tattha katamā nippesikatā?
rows.english: 64. ”Herein, what is belittling?

rows.pali: Lābhasakkārasilokasannissitassa pāpicchassa icchāpakatassa yā paresaṃ akkosanā vambhanā garahanā ukkhepanā samukkhepanā khipanā saṃkhipanā pāpanā sampāpanā avaṇṇahārikā parapiṭṭhimaṃsikatā, ayaṃ vuccati nippesikatā.
rows.english: Abusing of others, disparaging, reproaching, snubbing, continual snubbing, ridicule, continual ridicule, denigration, continual denigration, tale-bearing, backbiting, on the part of one bent on gain, honour and renown, of one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes—this is called belittling.

rows.pali: "Tattha katamā lābhena lābhaṃ nijigīsanatā?
rows.english: 65.”Herein, what is pursuing gain with gain?

rows.pali: Lābhasakkārasilokasannissito pāpiccho icchāpakato ito laddhaṃ āmisaṃ amutra harati, amutra vā laddhaṃ āmisaṃ idha āharati.
rows.english: Seeking, seeking for, seeking out, going in search of, searching for, searching out material goods by means of material goods, such as carrying there goods that have been got from here,

rows.pali: Yā evarūpā āmisena āmisassa eṭṭhi gaveṭṭhi pariyeṭṭhi esanā gavesanā pariyesanā, ayaṃ vuccati lābhena lābhaṃ nijigīsanatā"ti (vibha. 862-865).
rows.english: or carrying here goods that have been got from there, by one bent on gain, honour and renown, by one of evil wishes, a prey to wishes—this is called pursuing gain with gain.”19 (Vibh 352–53)

rows.pali: 17.Imissā pana pāḷiyā evamattho veditabbo.
rows.english: 66.The meaning of this text should be understood as follows:

rows.pali: Kuhananiddese tāva lābhasakkārasilokasannissitassāti lābhañca sakkārañca kittisaddañca sannissitassa, patthayantassāti attho.
rows.english: Firstly, as regards description of scheming: on the part of one bent on gain, honour and renown is on the part of one who is bent on gain, on honour, and on reputation; on the part of one who longs for them, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Pāpicchassāti asantaguṇadīpanakāmassa.
rows.english: Of one of evil wishes: of one who wants to show qualities that he has not got.

rows.pali: Icchāpakatassāti icchāya apakatassa, upaddutassāti attho.
rows.english: A prey to wishes:20 the meaning is, of one who is attacked by them.

rows.pali: Ito paraṃ yasmā paccayapaṭisevanasāmantajappanairiyāpathasannissitavasena mahāniddese tividhaṃ kuhanavatthu āgataṃ.
rows.english: And after this the passage beginning or by what is called rejection of requisites is given in order to show the three instances of scheming given in the Mahāniddesa as rejection of requisites, indirect talk, and that based on deportment.

rows.pali: Tasmā tividhampetaṃ dassetuṃ paccayapaṭisevanasaṅkhātena vāti evamādi āraddhaṃ.

Tattha cīvarādīhi nimantitassa tadatthikasseva sato pāpicchataṃ nissāya paṭikkhipanena, te ca gahapatike attani suppatiṭṭhitasaddhe ñatvā puna tesaṃ "aho ayyo appiccho na kiñci paṭiggaṇhituṃ icchati, suladdhaṃ vata no assa sace appamattakampi kiñci paṭiggaṇheyyā"ti nānāvidhehi upāyehi paṇītāni cīvarādīni upanentānaṃ tadanuggahakāmataṃyeva āvikatvā paṭiggahaṇena ca tato pabhuti api sakaṭabhārehi upanāmanahetubhūtaṃ vimhāpanaṃ paccayapaṭisevanasaṅkhātaṃ kuhanavatthūti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 67.Herein, [a bhikkhu] is invited to accept robes, etc., and, precisely because he wants them, he refuses them out of evil wishes. And then, since he knows that those householders believe in him implicitly when they think, “Oh, how few are our lord’s wishes! He will not accept a thing! ” and they put fine robes, etc., before him by various means, he then accepts, making a show that he wants to be compassionate towards them—it is this hypocrisy of his, which becomes the cause of their subsequently bringing them even by cartloads, that should be understood as the instance of scheming called rejection of requisites.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ mahāniddese –
rows.english: 68.For this is said in the Mahāniddesa:

rows.pali: Pāpicchasseva pana sato uttarimanussadhammādhigamaparidīpanavācāya tathā tathā vimhāpanaṃ sāmantajappanasaṅkhātaṃ kuhanavatthūti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 69.It is hypocrisy on the part of one of evil wishes, who gives it to be understood verbally in some way or other that he has attained a higher than human state, that should be understood as the instance of scheming called indirect talk,

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Pāpicchasseva pana sato sambhāvanādhippāyakatena iriyāpathena vimhāpanaṃ iriyāpathasannissitaṃ kuhanavatthūti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 70.It is hypocrisy on the part of one of evil wishes, which takes the form of deportment influenced by eagerness to be admired, that should be understood as the instance of scheming dependent on deportment,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "katamaṃ iriyāpathasaṅkhātaṃ kuhanavatthu.
rows.english: according as it is said: “What is the instance of scheming called deportment?

rows.pali: Idhekacco pāpiccho icchāpakato sambhāvanādhippāyo 'evaṃ maṃ jano sambhāvessatī'ti gamanaṃ saṇṭhapeti, ṭhānaṃ saṇṭhapeti, nisajjaṃ saṇṭhapeti, sayanaṃ saṇṭhapeti, paṇidhāya gacchati, paṇidhāya tiṭṭhati, paṇidhāya nisīdati, paṇidhāya seyyaṃ kappeti, samāhito viya gacchati, samāhito viya tiṭṭhati, nisīdati, seyyaṃ kappeti, āpāthakajjhāyī ca hoti, yā evarūpā iriyāpathassa aṭṭhapanā ṭhapanā saṇṭhapanā bhākuṭikā bhākuṭiyaṃ kuhanā kuhāyanā kuhitattaṃ, idaṃ vuccati iriyāpathasaṅkhātaṃ kuhanavatthū"ti (mahāni. 87).
rows.english: Here someone of evil wishes, a prey to wishes, eager to be admired, [thinking] ‘Thus people will admire me,’ composes his way of walking, composes his way of lying down; he walks studiedly, stands studiedly, sits studiedly, lies down studiedly; he walks as though concentrated, stands, sits, lies down as though concentrated; and he is one who meditates in public. Such disposing, posing, composing, of deportment, grimacing, grimacery, scheming, schemery, schemedness, is known as the instance of scheming called deportment” (Nidd I 225–26).

rows.pali: Tattha paccayapaṭisevanasaṅkhātenāti paccayapaṭisevananti evaṃ saṅkhātena paccayapaṭisevanena vā saṅkhātena.
rows.english: 71.Herein, the words by what is called rejection of requisites (§61) mean: by what is called thus “rejection of requisites”; or they mean: by means of the rejection of requisites that is so called.

rows.pali: Sāmantajappitenāti samīpabhaṇitena.
rows.english: By indirect talk means: by talking near to the subject.

rows.pali: Iriyāpathassa vāti catuiriyāpathassa.
rows.english: Of deportment means: of the four modes of deportment (postures).

rows.pali: Aṭṭhapanātiādi ṭhapanā, ādarena vā ṭhapanā.
rows.english: Disposing is initial posing, or careful posing.

rows.pali: Ṭhapanāti ṭhapanākāro.
rows.english: Posing is the manner of posing.

rows.pali: Saṇṭhapanāti abhisaṅkharaṇā, pāsādikabhāvakaraṇanti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Composing is prearranging; assuming a trust-inspiring attitude, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Bhākuṭikāti padhānapurimaṭṭhitabhāvadassanena bhākuṭikaraṇaṃ, mukhasaṅkocoti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Grimacing is making grimaces by showing great intenseness; facial contraction is what is meant.

rows.pali: Bhākuṭikaraṇaṃ sīlamassāti bhākuṭiko.
rows.english: One who has the habit of making grimaces is a grimacer.

rows.pali: Bhākuṭikassa bhāvo bhākuṭiyaṃ.
rows.english: The grimacer’s state is grimacery.

rows.pali: Kuhanāti vimhāpanā.
rows.english: Scheming is hypocrisy.

rows.pali: Kuhassa āyanā kuhāyanā.
rows.english: The way (āyanā) of a schemer (kuha) is schemery (kuhāyanā).

rows.pali: Kuhitassa bhāvo kuhitattanti.
rows.english: The state of what is schemed is schemedness.

rows.pali: Lapanāniddese ālapanāti vihāraṃ āgate manusse disvā "kimatthāya bhonto āgatā, kiṃ bhikkhū nimantituṃ, yadi evaṃ gacchatha re, ahaṃ pacchato pattaṃ gahetvā āgacchāmī"ti evaṃ āditova lapanā.
rows.english: 72.In the description of talking: talking at is talking thus on seeing people coming to the monastery, “What have you come for, good people? What, to invite bhikkhus? If it is that, then go along and I shall come later with [my bowl],” etc.;

rows.pali: Atha vā attānaṃ upanetvā "ahaṃ tisso, mayi rājā pasanno, mayi asuko ca asuko ca rājamahāmatto pasanno"ti evaṃ attupanāyikā lapanā ālapanā.
rows.english: or alternatively, talking at is talking by advertising oneself thus, “I am Tissa, the king trusts me, such and such king’s ministers trust me.”

rows.pali: Lapanāti puṭṭhassa sato vuttappakārameva lapanaṃ.
rows.english: Talking is the same kind of talking on being asked a question.

rows.pali: Sallapanāti gahapatikānaṃ ukkaṇṭhane bhītassa okāsaṃ datvā datvā suṭṭhu lapanā.
rows.english: Talking round is roundly talking by one who is afraid of householders’ displeasure because he has given occasion for it.

rows.pali: Ullapanāti mahākuṭumbiko mahānāviko mahādānapatīti evaṃ uddhaṃ katvā lapanā.
rows.english: Talking up is talking by extolling people thus, “He is a great land-owner, a great ship-owner, a great lord of giving.”

rows.pali: Samullapanāti sabbatobhāgena uddhaṃ katvā lapanā.
rows.english: Continual talking up is talking by extolling [people] in all ways.

rows.pali: Unnahanāti "upāsakā pubbe īdise kāle navadānaṃ detha, idāni kiṃ na dethā"ti evaṃ yāva "dassāma, bhante, okāsaṃ na labhāmā"tiādīni vadanti, tāva uddhaṃ uddhaṃ nahanā, veṭhanāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 73.Persuading is progressively involving23 [people] thus, “Lay followers, formerly you used to give first-fruit alms at such a time; why do you not do so now? ” until they say, “We shall give, venerable sir, we have had no opportunity,” etc.; entangling, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Atha vā ucchuhatthaṃ disvā "kuto ābhataṃ upāsakā"ti pucchati.
rows.english: Or alternatively, seeing someone with sugarcane in his hand, he asks, “Where are you coming from, lay follower?”

rows.pali: Ucchukhettato, bhanteti.
rows.english: —”From the sugarcane field, venerable sir”

rows.pali: Kiṃ tattha ucchu madhuranti.
rows.english: —”Is the sugarcane sweet there?”

rows.pali: Khāditvā, bhante, jānitabbanti.
rows.english: —”One can find out by eating, venerable sir”

rows.pali: "Na, upāsaka, bhikkhussa ucchuṃ dethā"ti vattuṃ vaṭṭatīti.
rows.english: —”It is not allowed, lay follower, for bhikkhus to say ‘Give [me some] sugarcane.’

rows.pali: Yā evarūpā nibbeṭhentassāpi veṭhanakathā, sā unnahanā.
rows.english: ” Such entangling talk from such an entangler is persuading.

rows.pali: Sabbatobhāgena punappunaṃ unnahanā samunnahanā.
rows.english: Persuading again and again in all ways is continual persuading.

rows.pali: Ukkācanāti "etaṃ kulaṃ maṃyeva jānāti.
rows.english: 74.Suggesting is insinuating by specifying thus, “That family alone understands me;

rows.pali: Sace ettha deyyadhammo uppajjati, mayhameva detī"ti evaṃ ukkhipitvā kācanā ukkācanā, uddīpanāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: if there is anything to be given there, they give it to me only”; pointing to, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Telakandarikavatthu cettha vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: And here the story of the oil-seller should be told.24

rows.pali: Sabbatobhāgena pana punappunaṃ ukkācanā samukkācanā.
rows.english: Suggesting in all ways again and again is continual suggesting.

rows.pali: Anuppiyabhāṇitāti saccānurūpaṃ dhammānurūpaṃ vā anapaloketvā punappunaṃ piyabhaṇanameva.
rows.english: 75.Ingratiating chatter is endearing chatter repeated again and again without regard to whether it is in conformity with truth and Dhamma.

rows.pali: Cāṭukamyatāti nīcavuttitā attānaṃ heṭṭhato heṭṭhato ṭhapetvā vattanaṃ.
rows.english: Flattery is speaking humbly, always maintaining an attitude of inferiority.

rows.pali: Muggasūpyatāti muggasūpasadisatā.
rows.english: Bean-soupery is resemblance to bean soup;

rows.pali: Yathā hi muggesu paccamānesu kocideva na paccati, avasesā paccanti, evaṃ yassa puggalassa vacane kiñcideva saccaṃ hoti, sesaṃ alīkaṃ, ayaṃ puggalo muggasūpyoti vuccati.
rows.english: for just as when beans are being cooked only a few do not get cooked, the rest get cooked, so too the person in whose speech only a little is true, the rest being false, is called a “bean soup”;

rows.pali: Tassa bhāvo muggasūpyatā.
rows.english: his state is bean-soupery.

rows.pali: Pāribhaṭyatāti pāribhaṭyabhāvo.
rows.english: 76. Fondling is the state of the act of fondling.

rows.pali: Yo hi kuladārake dhāti viya aṅkena vā khandhena vā paribhaṭati, dhāretīti attho.
rows.english: For when a man fondles children on his lap or on his shoulder like a nurse—he nurses, is the meaning—

rows.pali: Tassa paribhaṭassa kammaṃ pāribhaṭyuṃ.
rows.english: that fondler’s act is the act of fondling.

rows.pali: Pāribhaṭyassa bhāvo pāribhaṭyatāti.
rows.english: The state of the act of fondling is fondling.

rows.pali: Nemittikatāniddese nimittanti yaṃkiñci paresaṃ paccayadānasaññājanakaṃ kāyavacīkammaṃ.
rows.english: 77.In the description of hinting (nemittikatā): a sign (nimitta) is any bodily or verbal act that gets others to give requisites.

rows.pali: Nimittakammanti khādanīyaṃ gahetvā gacchante disvā "kiṃ khādanīyaṃ labhitthā"tiādinā nayena nimittakaraṇaṃ.
rows.english: Giving a sign is making a sign such as “What have you got to eat? ”, etc., on seeing [people] going along with food.

rows.pali: Obhāsoti paccayapaṭisaṃyuttakathā.
rows.english: Indication is talk that alludes to requisites.

rows.pali: Obhāsakammanti vacchapālake disvā "kiṃ ime vacchā khīragovacchā udāhu takkagovacchā"ti pucchitvā "khīragovacchā, bhante"ti vutte "na khīragovacchā, yadi khīragovacchā siyuṃ, bhikkhūpi khīraṃ labheyyu"nti evamādinā nayena tesaṃ dārakānaṃ mātāpitūnaṃ nivedetvā khīradāpanādikaṃ obhāsakaraṇaṃ.
rows.english: Giving indication: on seeing cowboys, he asks, “Are these milk cows’ calves or buttermilk cows’ calves? ” and when it is said, “They are milk cows’ calves, venerable sir,” [he remarks] “They are not milk cows’ calves. If they were milk cows’ calves the bhikkhus would be getting milk,” etc.; and his getting it to the knowledge of the boys’ parents in this way, and so making them give milk, is giving indication.

rows.pali: Sāmantajappāti samīpaṃ katvā jappanaṃ.
rows.english: 78.Indirect talk is talk that keeps near [to the subject].

rows.pali: Kulūpakabhikkhu vatthu cettha vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: And here there should be told the story of the bhikkhu supported by a family.

rows.pali: Kulūpako kira bhikkhu bhuñjitukāmo gehaṃ pavisitvā nisīdi.
rows.english: A bhikkhu, it seems, who was supported by a family went into the house wanting to eat and sat down.

rows.pali: Taṃ disvā adātukāmā gharaṇī "taṇḍulā natthī"ti bhaṇantī taṇḍule āharitukāmā viya paṭivissakagharaṃ gatā.
rows.english: The mistress of the house was unwilling to give. On seeing him she said, “There is no rice,” and she went to a neighbour’s house as though to get rice.

rows.pali: Bhikkhupi antogabbhaṃ pavisitvā olokento kavāṭakoṇe ucchuṃ, bhājane guḷaṃ, piṭake loṇamacchaphāle, kumbhiyaṃ taṇḍule, ghaṭe ghataṃ disvā nikkhamitvā nisīdi.
rows.english: The bhikkhu went into the storeroom. Looking round, he saw sugarcane in the corner behind the door, sugar in a bowl, a string of salt fish in a basket, rice in a jar, and ghee in a pot. He came out and sat down.

rows.pali: Gharaṇī "taṇḍule nālattha"nti āgatā.
rows.english: When the housewife came back, she said, “I did not get any rice.”

rows.pali: Bhikkhu "upāsike 'ajja bhikkhā na sampajjissatī'ti paṭikacceva nimittaṃ addasa"nti āha.
rows.english: The bhikkhu said, “Lay follower, I saw a sign just now that alms will not be easy to get today.”

rows.pali: Kiṃ, bhanteti.
rows.english: —“What, venerable sir?”

rows.pali: Kavāṭakoṇe nikkhittaṃ ucchuṃ viya sappaṃ addasaṃ, 'taṃ paharissāmī'ti olokento bhājane ṭhapitaṃ guḷapiṇḍaṃ viya pāsāṇaṃ, leḍḍukena pahaṭena sappena kataṃ piṭake nikkhittaloṇamacchaphālasadisaṃ phaṇaṃ, tassa taṃ leḍḍuṃ ḍaṃsitukāmassa kumbhiyā taṇḍulasadise dante, athassa kupitassa ghaṭe pakkhittaghatasadisaṃ mukhato nikkhamantaṃ visamissakaṃ kheḷanti.
rows.english: — ”I saw a snake that was like sugarcane put in the corner behind the door; looking for something to hit it with, I saw a stone like a lump of sugar in a bowl. When the snake had been hit with the clod, it spread out a hood like a string of salt fish in a basket, and its teeth as it tried to bite the clod were like rice grains in a jar. Then the saliva mixed with poison that came out to its mouth in its fury was like ghee put in a pot.”

rows.pali: Sā "na sakkā muṇḍakaṃ vañcetu"nti ucchuṃ datvā odanaṃ pacitvā ghataguḷamacchehi saddhiṃ sabbaṃ adāsīti.
rows.english: She thought, “There is no hoodwinking the shaveling,” so she gave him the sugarcane and she cooked the rice and gave it all to him with the ghee, the sugar and the fish.

rows.pali: Evaṃ samīpaṃ katvā jappanaṃ sāmantajappāti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 79.Such talk that keeps near [to the subject] should be understood as indirect talk.

rows.pali: Parikathāti yathā taṃ labhati tassa parivattetvā kathananti.
rows.english: Roundabout talk is talking round and round [the subject] as much as is allowed.

rows.pali: Nippesikatāniddese akkosanāti dasahi akkosavatthūhi akkosanaṃ.
rows.english: 80.In the description of belittling: abusing is abusing by means of the ten instances of abuse.25

rows.pali: Vambhanāti paribhavitvā kathanaṃ.
rows.english: Disparaging is contemptuous talk.

rows.pali: Garahaṇāti assaddho appasannotiādinā nayena dosāropanā.
rows.english: Reproaching is enumeration of faults such as “He is faithless, he is an unbeliever.”

rows.pali: Ukkhepanāti mā etaṃ ettha kathethāti vācāya ukkhipanaṃ.
rows.english: Snubbing is taking up verbally thus, “Don’t say that here.”

rows.pali: Sabbatobhāgena savatthukaṃ sahetukaṃ katvā ukkhepanā samukkhepanā.
rows.english: Snubbing in all ways, giving grounds and reasons, is continual snubbing.

rows.pali: Atha vā adentaṃ disvā "aho dānapatī"ti evaṃ ukkhipanaṃ ukkhepanā.
rows.english: Or alternatively, when someone does not give, taking him up thus, “Oh, the prince of givers!” is snubbing;

rows.pali: Mahādānapatīti evaṃ suṭṭhu ukkhepanā samukkhepanā.
rows.english: and the thorough snubbing thus, “A mighty prince of givers! ” is continual snubbing.

rows.pali: Khipanāti kiṃ imassa jīvitaṃ bījabhojinoti evaṃ uppaṇḍanā.
rows.english: Ridicule is making fun of someone thus, “What sort of a life has this man who eats up his seed [grain]?”

rows.pali: Saṃkhipanāti kiṃ imaṃ adāyakoti bhaṇatha, yo niccakālaṃ sabbesampi natthīti vacanaṃ detīti suṭṭhutaraṃ uppaṇḍanā.
rows.english: Continual ridicule is making fun of him more thoroughly thus, “What, you say this man is not a giver who always gives the words ‘There is nothing’ to everyone? ”

rows.pali: Pāpanāti adāyakattassa avaṇṇassa vā pāpanaṃ.
rows.english: 81.Denigration26 is denigrating someone by saying that he is not a giver, or by censuring him.

rows.pali: Sabbatobhāgena pāpanā sampāpanā.
rows.english: All-round denigration is continual denigration.

rows.pali: Avaṇṇahārikāti evaṃ me avaṇṇabhayāpi dassatīti gehato gehaṃ gāmato gāmaṃ janapadato janapadaṃ avaṇṇaharaṇaṃ.
rows.english: Tale-bearing is bearing tales from house to house, from village to village, from district to district, [thinking] “So they will give to me out of fear of my bearing tales.”

rows.pali: Parapiṭṭhimaṃsikatāti purato madhuraṃ bhaṇitvā parammukhe avaṇṇabhāsitā.
rows.english: Backbiting is speaking censoriously behind another’s back after speaking kindly to his face;

rows.pali: Esā hi abhimukhaṃ oloketuṃ asakkontassa parammukhānaṃ piṭṭhimaṃsaṃ khādanamiva hoti, tasmā parapiṭṭhimaṃsikatāti vuttā.
rows.english: for this is like biting the flesh of another’s back, when he is not looking, on the part of one who is unable to look him in the face; therefore it is called backbiting.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ vuccati nippesikatāti ayaṃ yasmā veḷupesikāya viya abbhaṅgaṃ parassa guṇaṃ nippeseti nipuñchati, yasmā vā gandhajātaṃ nipisitvā gandhamagganā viya paraguṇe nipisitvā vicuṇṇetvā esā lābhamagganā hoti, tasmā nippesikatāti vuccatīti.
rows.english: This is called belittling (nippesikatā) because it scrapes off (nippeseti), wipes off, the virtuous qualities of others as a bamboo scraper (veḷupesikā) does unguent, or because it is a pursuit of gain by grinding (nippiṃsitvā) and pulverizing others’ virtuous qualities, like the pursuit of perfume by grinding perfumed substances; that is why it is called belittling.

rows.pali: Lābhena lābhaṃ nijigīsanatāniddese nijigīsanatāti magganā.
rows.english: 82.`In the description of pursuing gain with gain: pursuing is hunting after.

rows.pali: Ito laddhanti imamhā gehā laddhaṃ.
rows.english: Got from here is got from this house.

rows.pali: Amutrāti amukamhi gehe.
rows.english: There is into that house.

rows.pali: Eṭṭhīti icchanā.
rows.english: Seeking is wanting.

rows.pali: Gaveṭṭhīti magganā.
rows.english: Seeking for is hunting after.

rows.pali: Pariyeṭṭhīti punappunaṃ magganā.
rows.english: Seeking out is hunting after again and again.

rows.pali: Ādito paṭṭhāya laddhaṃ laddhaṃ bhikkhaṃ tatra tatra kuladārakānaṃ datvā ante khīrayāguṃ labhitvā gatabhikkhuvatthu cettha kathetabbaṃ.
rows.english: The story of the bhikkhu who went round giving away the alms he had got at first to children of families here and there and in the end got milk and gruel should be told here.

rows.pali: Esanātiādīni eṭṭhiādīnameva vevacanāni, tasmā eṭṭhīti esanā.
rows.english: Searching, etc., are synonyms for “seeking,” etc., going in search of is seeking;

rows.pali: Gaveṭṭhīti gavesanā, pariyeṭṭhīti pariyesanā.
rows.english: searching for is seeking for; searching out is seeking out.

rows.pali: Iccevamettha yojanā veditabbā.
rows.english: and so the construction here should be understood thus (above).

rows.pali: Ayaṃ kuhanādīnaṃ attho.
rows.english: This is the meaning of scheming, and so on.

rows.pali: Idāni evamādīnañca pāpadhammānanti ettha ādisaddena "yathā vā paneke bhonto samaṇabrāhmaṇā saddhādeyyāni bhojanāni bhuñjitvā te evarūpāya tiracchānavijjāya micchājīvena jīvikaṃ kappenti.
rows.english: 83.Now, [as regards the words] The evil states beginning with (§42): here the words beginning with should be understood to include the many evil states given in the Brahmajāla Sutta in the way beginning, “Or just as some worthy ascetics, while eating the food given by the faithful, make a living by wrong livelihood, by such low arts as these,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, aṅgaṃ, nimittaṃ, uppātaṃ, supinaṃ, lakkhaṇaṃ, mūsikacchinnaṃ, aggihomaṃ, dabbihoma"nti (dī. ni. 1.21) ādinā nayena brahmajāle vuttānaṃ anekesaṃ pāpadhammānaṃ gahaṇaṃ veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: that is to say, by palmistry, by fortune-telling, by divining omens, by interpreting dreams, marks on the body, holes gnawed by mice; by fire sacrifice, by spoon oblation …” (D I 9).

rows.pali: Iti yvāyaṃ imesaṃ ājīvahetu paññattānaṃ channaṃ sikkhāpadānaṃ vītikkamavasena, imesañca "kuhanā lapanā nemittikatā nippesikatā lābhena lābhaṃ nijigīsanatā"ti evamādīnaṃ pāpadhammānaṃ vasena pavatto micchājīvo, yā tasmā sabbappakārāpi micchājīvā virati, idaṃ ājīvapārisuddhisīlaṃ.
rows.english: 84.So this wrong livelihood entails the transgression of these six training precepts announced on account of livelihood, and it entails the evil states beginning with “Scheming, talking, hinting, belittling, pursuing gain with gain.” And so it is the abstinence from all sorts of wrong livelihood that is virtue of livelihood purification,

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ vacanattho.
rows.english: the word-meaning of which is this:

rows.pali: Etaṃ āgamma jīvantīti ājīvo.
rows.english: on account of it they live, thus it is livelihood.

rows.pali: Ko so, paccayapariyesanavāyāmo.
rows.english: What is that? It is the effort consisting in the search for requisites.

rows.pali: Pārisuddhīti parisuddhatā.
rows.english: “Purification” is purifiedness.

rows.pali: Ājīvassa pārisuddhi ājīvapārisuddhi.
rows.english: “Livelihood purification” is purification of livelihood.

rows.pali: 18.Yaṃ panetaṃ tadanantaraṃ paccayasannissitasīlaṃ vuttaṃ, tattha paṭisaṅkhā yonisoti upāyena pathena paṭisaṅkhāya ñatvā, paccavekkhitvāti attho.
rows.english: 85.(d) As regards the next kind called virtue concerning requisites, Herein, reflecting wisely is reflecting as the means and as the way;27 by knowing, by reviewing, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Ettha ca sītassa paṭighātāyātiādinā nayena vuttapaccavekkhaṇameva "yoniso paṭisaṅkhā"ti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: And here it is the reviewing stated in the way beginning, “For protection from cold” that should be understood as “reflecting wisely.”

rows.pali: Tattha cīvaranti antaravāsakādīsu yaṃkiñci.
rows.english: 86. Herein, the robe is any one of those beginning with the inner cloth.

rows.pali: Paṭisevatīti paribhuñjati, nivāseti vā pārupati vā.
rows.english: He uses: he employs; dresses in [as inner cloth], or puts on [as upper garment].

rows.pali: Yāvadevāti payojanāvadhiparicchedaniyamavacanaṃ, ettakameva hi yogino cīvarapaṭisevane payojanaṃ yadidaṃ sītassa paṭighātāyātiādi, na ito bhiyyo.
rows.english: Only [31] is a phrase signifying invariability in the definition of a limit28 of a purpose; the purpose in the meditator’s making use of the robes is that much only, namely, protection from cold, etc., not more than that.

rows.pali: Sītassāti ajjhattadhātukkhobhavasena vā bahiddhāutupariṇāmanavasena vā uppannassa yassa kassaci sītassa.
rows.english: From cold: from any kind of cold arisen either through disturbance of elements internally or through change in temperature externally.

rows.pali: Paṭighātāyāti paṭihananatthaṃ.
rows.english: For protection: for the purpose of warding off;

rows.pali: Yathā sarīre ābādhaṃ na uppādeti, evaṃ tassa vinodanatthaṃ.
rows.english: for the purpose of eliminating it so that it may not arouse affliction in the body.

rows.pali: Sītabbhāhate hi sarīre vikkhittacitto yoniso padahituṃ na sakkoti, tasmā sītassa paṭighātāya cīvaraṃ paṭisevitabbanti bhagavā anuññāsi.
rows.english: For when the body is afflicted by cold, the distracted mind cannot be wisely exerted. That is why the Blessed One permitted the robe to be used for protection from cold.

rows.pali: Esa nayo sabbattha.
rows.english: So in each instance,

rows.pali: Kevalañhettha uṇhassāti aggisantāpassa.
rows.english: except that from heat means from the heat of fire,

rows.pali: Tassa vanadāhādīsu sambhavo veditabbo.
rows.english: the origin of which should be understood as forest fires, and so on.

rows.pali: Ḍaṃsamakasavātātapasarīsapasamphassānanti ettha pana ḍaṃsāti ḍaṃsanamakkhikā, andhamakkhikātipi vuccanti.
rows.english: 87.From contact with gadflies and flies, wind and burning and creeping things: here gadflies are flies that bite; they are also called “blind flies.”

rows.pali: Makasā makasā eva.
rows.english: Flies are just flies.

rows.pali: Vātāti sarajaarajādibhedā.
rows.english: Wind is distinguished as that with dust and that without dust.

rows.pali: Ātapoti sūriyātapo.
rows.english: Burning is burning of the sun.

rows.pali: Sarīsapāti ye keci sarantā gacchanti dīghajātikā sappādayo, tesaṃ daṭṭhasamphasso ca phuṭṭhasamphasso cāti duvidho samphasso, sopi cīvaraṃ pārupitvā nisinnaṃ na bādhati, tasmā tādisesu ṭhānesu tesaṃ paṭighātatthāya paṭisevati.
rows.english: Creeping things are any long creatures such as snakes and so on that move by crawling. Contact with them is of two kinds: contact by being bitten and contact by being touched. And that does not worry him who sits with a robe on. So he uses it for the purpose of protection from such things.

rows.pali: Yāvadevāti puna etassa vacanaṃ niyatapayojanāvadhiparicchedadassanatthaṃ, hirikopīnapaṭicchādanañhi niyatapayojanaṃ, itarāni kadāci kadāci honti.
rows.english: 88.Only: the word is repeated in order to define a subdivision of the invariable purpose; for the concealment of the private parts is an invariable purpose; the others are purposes periodically.

rows.pali: Tattha hirikopīnanti taṃ taṃ sambādhaṭṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, private parts are any parts of the pudendum.

rows.pali: Yasmiṃ yasmiñhi aṅge vivariyamāne hirī kuppati vinassati, taṃ taṃ hiriṃ kopanato hirikopīnanti vuccati.
rows.english: For when a member is disclosed, conscience (hiri) is disturbed (kuppati), offended. It is called “private parts” (hirikopīna) because of the disturbance of conscience (hiri-kopana).

rows.pali: Tassa ca hirikopīnassa paṭicchādanatthanti hirikopīnapaṭicchādanatthaṃ.
rows.english: For the purpose of concealing the private parts: for the purpose of the concealment of those private parts.

rows.pali: Hirikopīnaṃ paṭicchādanatthantipi pāṭho.
rows.english: [As well as the reading “hiriko-pīna-paṭicchādanatthaṃ] there is a reading “hirikopīnaṃ paṭicchādanatthaṃ. ”

rows.pali: Piṇḍapātanti yaṃkiñci āhāraṃ.
rows.english: 89.Alms food is any sort of food.

rows.pali: Yo hi koci āhāro bhikkhuno piṇḍolyena patte patitattā piṇḍapātoti vuccati.
rows.english: For any sort of nutriment is called “alms food” (piṇḍapāta—lit. “lump-dropping”) because of its having been dropped (patitattā) into a bhikkhu’s bowl during his alms round (piṇḍolya).

rows.pali: Piṇḍānaṃ vā pāto piṇḍapāto, tattha tattha laddhānaṃ bhikkhānaṃ sannipāto samūhoti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Or alms food (piṇḍapāta) is the dropping (pāta) of the lumps (piṇḍa); it is the concurrence (sannipāta), the collection, of alms (bhikkhā) obtained here and there, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Neva davāyāti na gāmadārakādayo viya davatthaṃ, kīḷānimittanti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Neither for amusement: neither for the purpose of amusement, as with village boys, etc.; for the sake of sport, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Na madāyāti na muṭṭhikamallādayo viya madatthaṃ, balamadanimittaṃ porisamadanimittañcāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Nor for intoxication: not for the purpose of intoxication, as with boxers, etc.; for the sake of intoxication with strength and for the sake of intoxication with manhood, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Na maṇḍanāyāti na antepurikavesiyādayo viya maṇḍanatthaṃ, aṅgapaccaṅgānaṃ pīṇabhāvanimittanti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Nor for smartening: not for the purpose of smartening, as with royal concubines, courtesans, etc.; for the sake of plumpness in all the limbs, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Na vibhūsanāyāti na naṭanaccakādayo viya vibhūsanatthaṃ, pasannacchavivaṇṇatānimittanti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Nor for embellishment: not for the purpose of embellishment, as with actors, dancers, etc.; for the sake of a clear skin and complexion, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Ettha ca neva davāyāti etaṃ mohūpanissayappahānatthaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 90. And here the clause neither for amusement is stated for the purpose of abandoning support for delusion;

rows.pali: Na madāyāti etaṃ dosūpanissayappahānatthaṃ.
rows.english: nor for intoxication is said for the purpose of abandoning support for hate;

rows.pali: Na maṇḍanāya na vibhūsanāyāti etaṃ rāgūpanissayappahānatthaṃ.
rows.english: nor for smartening nor for embellishment is said for the purpose of abandoning support for greed.

rows.pali: Neva davāya na madāyāti cetaṃ attano saṃyojanuppattipaṭisedhanatthaṃ.
rows.english: And neither for amusement nor for intoxication is said for the purpose of preventing the arising of fetters for oneself.

rows.pali: Na maṇḍanāya na vibhūsanāyāti etaṃ parassapi saṃyojanuppattipaṭisedhanatthaṃ.
rows.english: Nor for smartening nor for embellishment is said for the purpose of preventing the arising of fetters for another.

rows.pali: Catūhipi cetehi ayoniso paṭipattiyā kāmasukhallikānuyogassa ca pahānaṃ vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: And the abandoning of both unwise practice and devotion to indulgence of sense pleasures should be understood as stated by these four.

rows.pali: Yāvadevāti vuttatthameva.
rows.english: Only has the meaning already stated.

rows.pali: Imassa kāyassāti etassa catumahābhūtikassa rūpakāyassa.
rows.english: 91.Of this body: of this material body consisting of the four great primaries.

rows.pali: Ṭhitiyāti pabandhaṭṭhitatthaṃ.
rows.english: For the endurance: for the purpose of continued endurance.

rows.pali: Yāpanāyāti pavattiyā avicchedatthaṃ, cirakālaṭṭhitatthaṃ vā.
rows.english: And continuance: for the purpose of not interrupting [life’s continued] occurrence, or for the purpose of endurance for a long time.

rows.pali: Gharūpatthambhamiva hi jiṇṇagharasāmiko, akkhabbhañjanamiva ca sākaṭiko kāyassa ṭhitatthaṃ yāpanatthañcesa piṇḍapātaṃ paṭisevati, na davamadamaṇḍanavibhūsanatthaṃ.
rows.english: He makes use of the alms food for the purpose of the endurance, for the purpose of the continuance, of the body, as the owner of an old house uses props for his house, and as a carter uses axle grease, not for the purpose of amusement, intoxication, smartening, and embellishment.

rows.pali: Apica ṭhitīti jīvitindriyassetaṃ adhivacanaṃ, tasmā imassa kāyassa ṭhitiyā yāpanāyāti ettāvatā etassa kāyassa jīvitindriyapavattāpanatthantipi vuttaṃ hotīti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: Furthermore, endurance is a term for the life faculty. So what has been said as far as the words for the endurance and continuance of this body can be understood to mean: for the purpose of maintaining the occurrence of the life faculty in this body.

rows.pali: Vihiṃsūparatiyāti vihiṃsā nāma jighacchā ābādhaṭṭhena.
rows.english: 92. For the ending of discomfort: hunger is called “discomfort” in the sense of afflicting.

rows.pali: Tassā uparamatthampesa piṇḍapātaṃ paṭisevati, vaṇālepanamiva uṇhasītādīsu tappaṭikāraṃ viya ca.
rows.english: He makes use of alms food for the purpose of ending that, like anointing a wound, like counteracting heat with cold, and so on.

rows.pali: Brahmacariyānuggahāyāti sakalasāsanabrahmacariyassa ca maggabrahmacariyassa ca anuggahatthaṃ.
rows.english: For assisting the life of purity: for the purpose of assisting the life of purity consisting in the whole dispensation and the life of purity consisting in the path.

rows.pali: Ayañhi piṇḍapātapaṭisevanapaccayā kāyabalaṃ nissāya sikkhattayānuyogavasena bhavakantāranittharaṇatthaṃ paṭipajjanto brahmacariyānuggahāya paṭisevati, kantāranittharaṇatthikā puttamaṃsaṃ (saṃ. ni. 2.63) viya, nadīnittharaṇatthikā kullaṃ (ma. ni. 1.240) viya, samuddanittharaṇatthikā nāvamiva ca.
rows.english: For while this [bhikkhu] is engaged in crossing the desert of existence by means of devotion to the three trainings depending on bodily strength whose necessary condition is the use of alms food, he makes use of it to assist the life of purity just as those seeking to cross the desert used their child’s flesh,29 just as those seeking to cross a river use a raft, and just as those seeking to cross the ocean use a ship.

rows.pali: Itipurāṇañca vedanaṃ paṭihaṅkhāmi navañca vedanaṃ na uppādessāmīti etaṃ iminā piṇḍapātapaṭisevanena purāṇañca jighacchāvedanaṃ paṭihaṅkhāmi, navañca vedanaṃ aparimitabhojanapaccayaṃ āharahatthakaalaṃsāṭakatatravaṭṭakakākamāsakabhuttavamitakabrāhmaṇānaṃ aññataro viya na uppādessāmītipi paṭisevati, bhesajjamiva gilāno.
rows.english: 93.Thus I shall put a stop to old feelings and shall not arouse new feelings: [33] thus as a sick man uses medicine, he uses [alms food, thinking]: “By use of this alms food I shall put a stop to the old feeling of hunger, and I shall not arouse a new feeling by immoderate eating, like one of the [proverbial] brahmans, that is, one who eats till he has to be helped up by hand, or till his clothes will not meet, or till he rolls there [on the ground], or till crows can peck from his mouth, or until he vomits what he has eaten.

rows.pali: Atha vā yā adhunā asappāyāparimitabhojanaṃ nissāya purāṇakammapaccayavasena uppajjanato purāṇavedanāti vuccati.
rows.english: Or alternatively, there is that which is called ‘old feelings’ because, being conditioned by former kamma, it arises now in dependence on unsuitable immoderate eating—

rows.pali: Sappāyaparimitabhojanena tassā paccayaṃ vināsento taṃ purāṇañca vedanaṃ paṭihaṅkhāmi.
rows.english: I shall put a stop to that old feeling, forestalling its condition by suitable moderate eating.

rows.pali: Yā cāyaṃ adhunā kataṃ ayuttaparibhogakammūpacayaṃ nissāya āyatiṃ uppajjanato navavedanāti vuccati.
rows.english: And there is that which is called ‘new feeling’ because it will arise in the future in dependence on the accumulation of kamma consisting in making improper use [of the requisite of alms food] now—

rows.pali: Yuttaparibhogavasena tassā mūlaṃ anibbattento taṃ navañca vedanaṃ na uppādessāmīti evampettha attho daṭṭhabbo.
rows.english: I shall also not arouse that new feeling, avoiding by means of proper use the production of its root.” This is how the meaning should be understood here.

rows.pali: Ettāvatā yuttaparibhogasaṅgaho attakilamathānuyogappahānaṃ dhammikasukhāpariccāgo ca dīpito hotīti veditabbo.
rows.english: What has been shown so far can be understood to include proper use [of requisites], abandoning of devotion to self-mortification, and not giving up lawful bliss (pleasure).

rows.pali: Yātrā ca me bhavissatīti parimitaparibhogena jīvitindriyupacchedakassa iriyāpathabhañjakassa vā parissayassa abhāvato cirakālagamanasaṅkhātā yātrā ca me bhavissati imassa paccayāyattavuttino kāyassātipi paṭisevati, yāpyarogī viya tappaccayaṃ.
rows.english: 94.And I shall be healthy: “In this body, which exists in dependence on requisites, I shall, by moderate eating, have health called ‘long endurance’ since there will be no danger of severing the life faculty or interrupting the [continuity of the] postures. ” [Reflecting] in this way, he makes use [of the alms food] as a sufferer from a chronic disease does of his medicine.

rows.pali: Anavajjatā ca phāsuvihāro cāti ayuttapariyesanapaṭiggahaṇaparibhogaparivajjanena anavajjatā, parimitaparibhogena phāsuvihāro.
rows.english: And blameless and live in comfort (lit. “and have blamelessness and a comfortable abiding”): he makes use of them thinking: “I shall have blamelessness by avoiding improper search, acceptance and eating, and I shall have a comfortable abiding by moderate eating.”

rows.pali: Asappāyāparimitaparibhogapaccayā aratitandīvijambhitā. Viññūgarahādidosābhāvena vā anavajjatā, sappāyaparimitabhojanapaccayā kāyabalasambhavena phāsuvihāro.
rows.english: Or he does so thinking: “I shall have blamelessness due to absence of such faults as boredom, sloth, sleepiness, blame by the wise, etc., that have unseemly immoderate eating as their condition; and I shall have a comfortable abiding by producing bodily strength that has seemly moderate eating as its condition.”

rows.pali: Yāvadatthaudarāvadehakabhojanaparivajjanena vā seyyasukhapassasukhamiddhasukhānaṃ pahānato anavajjatā, catupañcālopamattaūnabhojanena catuiriyāpathayogyabhāvapaṭipādanato phāsuvihāro ca me bhavissatītipi paṭisevati.
rows.english: Or he does so thinking: “I shall have blamelessness by abandoning the pleasure of lying down, lolling and torpor, through refraining from eating as much as possible to stuff the belly; and I shall have a comfortable abiding by controlling the four postures through eating four or five mouthfuls less than the maximum.”

rows.pali: Vuttampi hetaṃ –
rows.english: For this is said:

rows.pali: Ettāvatā ca payojanapariggaho majjhimā ca paṭipadā dīpitā hotīti veditabbā.
rows.english: Now, what has been shown at this point can be understood as discernment of purpose and practice of the middle way.

rows.pali: Senāsananti senañca āsanañca.
rows.english: 95.Resting place (senāsana): this is the bed (sena) and seat (āsana).

rows.pali: Yattha yattha hi seti vihāre vā aḍḍhayogādimhi vā, taṃ senaṃ.
rows.english: For wherever one sleeps (seti), whether in a monastery or in a lean-to, etc., that is the bed (sena);

rows.pali: Yattha yattha āsati nisīdati, taṃ āsanaṃ.
rows.english: wherever one seats oneself (āsati), sits (nisīdati), that is the seat (āsana).

rows.pali: Taṃ ekato katvā senāsananti vuccati.
rows.english: Both together are called “resting-place” (or “abode”—senāsana).

rows.pali: Utuparissayavinodanapaṭisallānārāmatthanti parisahanaṭṭhena utuyeva utuparissayo.
rows.english: For the purpose of warding off the perils of climate and enjoying retreat: the climate itself in the sense of imperilling (parisahana) is “perils of climate” (utu-parissaya).

rows.pali: Utuparissayassa vinodanatthañca paṭisallānārāmatthañca. Yo sarīrābādhacittavikkhepakaro asappāyo utu senāsanapaṭisevanena vinodetabbo hoti, tassa vinodanatthaṃ ekībhāvasukhatthañcāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Unsuitable climatic conditions that cause mental distraction due to bodily affliction can be warded off by making use of the resting place; it is for the purpose of warding off these and for the purpose of the pleasure of solitude, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Kāmañca sītapaṭighātādināva utuparissayavinodanaṃ vuttameva.
rows.english: Of course, the warding off of the perils of climate is stated by [the phrase] “protection from cold,” etc., too;

rows.pali: Yathā pana cīvarapaṭisevane hirikopīnapaṭicchādanaṃ niyatapayojanaṃ, itarāni kadāci kadāci bhavantīti vuttaṃ, evamidhāpi niyataṃ utuparissayavinodanaṃ sandhāya idaṃ vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: but, just as in the case of making use of the robes the concealment of the private parts is stated as an invariable purpose while the others are periodical [purposes], so here also this [last] should be understood as mentioned with reference to the invariable warding off of the perils of climate.

rows.pali: Atha vā ayaṃ vuttappakāro utu utuyeva.
rows.english: Or alternatively, this “climate” of the kind stated is just climate;

rows.pali: Parissayo pana duvidho pākaṭaparissayo ca, paṭicchannaparissayo ca (mahāni. 5).
rows.english: but “perils” are of two kinds: evident perils and concealed perils (see Nidd I 12).

rows.pali: Tattha pākaṭaparissayo sīhabyagghādayo.
rows.english: Herein, evident perils are lions, tigers, etc.,

rows.pali: Paṭicchannaparissayo rāgadosādayo.
rows.english: while concealed perils are greed, hate, and so on.

rows.pali: Ye yattha apariguttiyā ca asappāyarūpadassanādinā ca ābādhaṃ na karonti, taṃ senāsanaṃ evaṃ jānitvā paccavekkhitvā paṭisevanto bhikkhu paṭisaṅkhā yoniso senāsanaṃ utuparissayavinodanatthaṃ paṭisevatīti veditabbo.
rows.english: When a bhikkhu knows and reflects thus in making use of the kind of resting place where these [perils] do not, owing to unguarded doors and sight of unsuitable visible objects, etc., cause affliction, he can be understood as one who “reflecting wisely makes use of the resting place for the purpose of warding off the perils of climate.”

rows.pali: Gilānapaccayabhesajjaparikkhāranti ettha rogassa paṭiayanaṭṭhena paccayo, paccanīkagamanaṭṭhenāti attho.
rows.english: 96.The requisite of medicine as cure for the sick: here “cure” (paccaya = going against) is in the sense of going against (pati-ayana) illness; in the sense of countering, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Yassa kassaci sappāyassetaṃ adhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: This is a term for any suitable remedy.

rows.pali: Bhisakkassa kammaṃ tena anuññātattāti bhesajjaṃ.
rows.english: It is the medical man’s work (bhisakkassa kammaṃ) because it is permitted by him, thus it is medicine (bhesajja).

rows.pali: Gilānapaccayova bhesajjaṃ gilānapaccayabhesajjaṃ, yaṃkiñci gilānassa sappāyaṃ bhisakkakammaṃ telamadhuphāṇitādīti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Or the cure for the sick itself as medicine is “medicine as cure for the sick. ” Any work of a medical man such as oil, honey, ghee, etc., that is suitable for one who is sick, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Parikkhāroti pana "sattahi nagaraparikkhārehi suparikkhataṃ hotī"ti (a. ni. 7.67) ādīsu parivāro vuccati.
rows.english: A “requisite” (parikkhāra), however, in such passages as “It is well supplied with the requisites of a city” (A IV 106) is equipment;

rows.pali: "Ratho sīlaparikkhāro, jhānakkho cakkavīriyo"ti (saṃ. ni. 5.4) ādīsu alaṅkāro.
rows.english: in such passages as “The chariot has the requisite of virtue, the axle of jhāna, the wheel of energy” (S V 6) [35] it is an ornament;

rows.pali: "Ye ca kho ime pabbajitena jīvitaparikkhārā samudānetabbā"ti (ma. ni. 1.191-192) ādīsu sambhāro.
rows.english: in such passages as “The requisites for the life of one who has gone into homelessness that should be available” (M I 104), it is an accessory.

rows.pali: Idha pana sambhāropi parivāropi vaṭṭati.
rows.english: But here both equipment and accessory are applicable.

rows.pali: Tañhi gilānapaccayabhesajjaṃ jīvitassa parivāropi hoti, jīvitanāsakābādhuppattiyā antaraṃ adatvā rakkhaṇato sambhāropi.
rows.english: For that medicine as a cure for the sick is equipment for maintaining life because it protects by preventing the arising of affliction destructive to life;

rows.pali: Yathā ciraṃ pavattati, evamassa kāraṇabhāvato, tasmā parikkhāroti vuccati.
rows.english: and it is an accessory too because it is an instrument for prolonging life. That is why it is called “requisite.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ gilānapaccayabhesajjañca taṃ parikkhāro cāti gilānapaccayabhesajjaparikkhāro.
rows.english: So it is medicine as cure for the sick and that is a requisite, thus it is a “requisite of medicine as cure for the sick.”

rows.pali: Taṃ gilānapaccayabhesajjaparikkhāraṃ.
rows.english: [He makes use of] that requisite of medicine as cure for the sick;

rows.pali: Gilānassa yaṃkiñci sappāyaṃ bhisakkānuññātaṃ telamadhuphāṇitādi jīvitaparikkhāranti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: any requisite for life consisting of oil, honey, molasses, ghee, etc., that is allowed by a medical man as suitable for the sick, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Uppannānanti jātānaṃ bhūtānaṃ nibbattānaṃ.
rows.english: 97.From arisen: from born, become, produced.

rows.pali: Veyyābādhikānanti ettha byābādhoti dhātukkhobho, taṃsamuṭṭhānā ca kuṭṭhagaṇḍapīḷakādayo.
rows.english: Hurtful: here “hurt (affliction)” is a disturbance of elements, and it is the leprosy, tumours, boils, etc., originated by that disturbance.

rows.pali: Byābādhato uppannattā veyyābādhikā.
rows.english: Hurtful (veyyābādhika) because arisen in the form of hurt (byābādha).

rows.pali: Vedanānanti dukkhavedanā akusalavipākavedanā.
rows.english: Feelings: painful feelings, feelings resulting from unprofitable kamma—

rows.pali: Tāsaṃ veyyābādhikānaṃ vedanānaṃ.
rows.english: from those hurtful feelings.

rows.pali: Abyābajjhaparamatāyāti niddukkhaparamatāya.
rows.english: For complete immunity from affliction: for complete freedom from pain;

rows.pali: Yāva taṃ dukkhaṃ sabbaṃ pahīnaṃ hoti tāvāti attho.
rows.english: so that all that is painful is abandoned, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Evamidaṃ saṅkhepato paṭisaṅkhā yoniso paccayaparibhogalakkhaṇaṃ paccayasannissitasīlaṃ veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: This is how this virtue concerning requisites should be understood. In brief its characteristic is the use of requisites after wise reflection.

rows.pali: Vacanattho panettha – cīvarādayo hi yasmā te paṭicca nissāya paribhuñjamānā pāṇino ayanti pavattanti, tasmā paccayāti vuccanti.
rows.english: The word-meaning here is this: because breathing things go (ayanti), move, proceed, using [what they use] in dependence on these robes, etc., these robes, etc., are therefore called requisites (paccaya = ger. of paṭi + ayati);

rows.pali: Te paccaye sannissitanti paccayasannissitaṃ.
rows.english: “concerning requisites” is concerning those requisites.

rows.pali: 19.Evametasmiṃ catubbidhe sīle saddhāya pātimokkhasaṃvaro sampādetabbo.
rows.english: 98.(a) So, in this fourfold virtue, Pātimokkha restraint has to be undertaken by means of faith.

rows.pali: Saddhāsādhano hi so, sāvakavisayātītattā sikkhāpadapaññattiyā.
rows.english: For that is accomplished by faith, since the announcing of training precepts is outside the disciples’ province;

rows.pali: Sikkhāpadapaññattiyācanapaṭikkhepo cettha nidassanaṃ.
rows.english: and the evidence here is the refusal of the request to [allow disciples to] announce training precepts (see Vin III 9–10).

rows.pali: Tasmā yathā paññattaṃ sikkhāpadaṃ anavasesaṃ saddhāya samādiyitvā jīvitepi apekkhaṃ akarontena sādhukaṃ sampādetabbaṃ.
rows.english: Having therefore undertaken through faith the training precepts without exception as announced, one should completely perfect them without regard for life.

rows.pali: Vuttampi hetaṃ –
rows.english: For this is said:

rows.pali: Aparampi vuttaṃ – "evameva kho pahārāda yaṃ mayā sāvakānaṃ sikkhāpadaṃ paññattaṃ, taṃ mama sāvakā jīvitahetupi nātikkamantī"ti (a. ni. 8.19).
rows.english: Also it is said further: “So too, sire, when a training precept for disciples is announced by me, my disciples do not transgress it even for the sake of life” (A IV 201).

rows.pali: Imasmiṃ ca panatthe aṭaviyaṃ corehi baddhatherānaṃ vatthūni veditabbāni.
rows.english: 99.And the story of the elders bound by robbers in the forest should be understood in this sense.

rows.pali: Mahāvattaniaṭaviyaṃ kira theraṃ corā kāḷavallīhi bandhitvā nipajjāpesuṃ.
rows.english: It seems that robbers in the Mahāvaṭṭanī Forest bound an elder with black creepers and made him lie down.

rows.pali: Thero yathānipannova sattadivasāni vipassanaṃ vaḍḍhetvā anāgāmiphalaṃ pāpuṇitvā tattheva kālaṃ katvā brahmaloke nibbatti.
rows.english: While he lay there for seven days he augmented his insight, and after reaching the fruition of non-return, he died there and was reborn in the Brahmā-world.

rows.pali: Aparampi theraṃ tambapaṇṇidīpe pūtilatāya bandhitvā nipajjāpesuṃ.
rows.english: Also they bound another elder in Tambapaṇṇi Island (Sri Lanka) with string creepers and made him lie down.

rows.pali: So vanadāhe āgacchante valliṃ acchinditvāva vipassanaṃ paṭṭhapetvā samasīsī hutvā parinibbāyi.
rows.english: When a forest fire came and the creepers were not cut, he established insight and attained Nibbāna simultaneously with his death.

rows.pali: Dīghabhāṇakaabhayatthero pañcahi bhikkhusatehi saddhiṃ āgacchanto disvā therassa sarīraṃ jhāpetvā cetiyaṃ kārāpesi.
rows.english: When the Elder Abhaya, a preacher of the Dīgha Nikāya, passed by with five hundred bhikkhus, he saw [what had happened] and he had the elder’s body cremated and a shrine built.

rows.pali: Tasmā aññopi saddho kulaputto –
rows.english: Therefore let other clansmen also:

rows.pali: Yathā ca pātimokkhasaṃvaro saddhāya, evaṃ satiyā indriyasaṃvaro sampādetabbo.
rows.english: 100.(b) And as Pātimokkha restraint is undertaken out of faith, so restraint of the sense faculties should be undertaken with mindfulness.

rows.pali: Satisādhano hi so, satiyā adhiṭṭhitānaṃ indriyānaṃ abhijjhādīhi ananvāssavanīyato.
rows.english: For that is accomplished by mindfulness, because when the sense faculties’ functions are founded on mindfulness, there is no liability to invasion by covetousness and the rest.

rows.pali: Tasmā "varaṃ, bhikkhave, tattāya ayosalākāya ādittāya sampajjalitāya sajotibhūtāya cakkhundriyaṃ sampalimaṭṭhaṃ, na tveva cakkhuviññeyyesu rūpesu anubyañjanaso nimittaggāho"ti (saṃ. ni. 4.235) ādinā nayena ādittapariyāyaṃ samanussaritvā rūpādīsu visayesu cakkhudvārādipavattassa viññāṇassa abhijjhādīhi anvāssavanīyaṃ nimittādiggāhaṃ asammuṭṭhāya satiyā nisedhentena esa sādhukaṃ sampādetabbo.
rows.english: So, recollecting the Fire Discourse, which begins thus, “Better, bhikkhus, the extirpation of the eye faculty by a red-hot burning blazing glowing iron spike than the apprehension of signs in the particulars of visible objects cognizable by the eye” (S IV 168), this [restraint] should be properly undertaken by preventing with unremitting mindfulness any apprehension, in the objective fields consisting of visible data, etc., of any signs, etc., likely to encourage covetousness, etc., to invade consciousness occurring in connection with the eye door, and so on.

rows.pali: Evaṃ asampādite hi etasmiṃ pātimokkhasaṃvarasīlampi anaddhaniyaṃ hoti aciraṭṭhitikaṃ, asaṃvihitasākhāparivāramiva sassaṃ.
rows.english: 101. When not undertaken thus, virtue of Pātimokkha restraint is unenduring: it does not last, like a crop not fenced in with branches.

rows.pali: Haññate cāyaṃ kilesacorehi, vivaṭadvāro viya gāmo parassa hārīhi.
rows.english: And it is raided by the robber defilements as a village with open gates is by thieves.

rows.pali: Cittañcassa rāgo samativijjhati, ducchannamagāraṃ vuṭṭhi viya.
rows.english: And lust leaks into his mind as rain does into a badly-roofed house.

rows.pali: Vuttampi hetaṃ –
rows.english: For this is said:

rows.pali: Sampādite pana tasmiṃ pātimokkhasaṃvarasīlampi addhaniyaṃ hoti ciraṭṭhitikaṃ, susaṃvihitasākhāparivāramiva sassaṃ.
rows.english: 102. When it is undertaken thus, virtue of Pātimokkha restraint is enduring: it lasts, like a crop well fenced in with branches.

rows.pali: Na haññate cāyaṃ kilesacorehi, susaṃvutadvāro viya gāmo parassa hārīhi.
rows.english: And it is not raided by the robber defilements, as a village with well-guarded gates is not by thieves.

rows.pali: Na cassa cittaṃ rāgo samativijjhati, succhannamagāraṃ vuṭṭhi viya.
rows.english: And lust does not leak into his mind, as rain does not into a well-roofed house.

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ –
rows.english: For this is said:

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pana atiukkaṭṭhadesanā.
rows.english: 103. This, however, is the teaching at its very highest.

rows.pali: Cittaṃ nāmetaṃ lahuparivattaṃ, tasmā uppannaṃ rāgaṃ asubhamanasikārena vinodetvā indriyasaṃvaro sampādetabbo, adhunāpabbajitena vaṅgīsattherena viya.
rows.english: This mind is called “quickly transformed” (A I 10), so restraint of the faculties should be undertaken by removing arisen lust with the contemplation of foulness, as was done by the Elder Vaṅgīsa soon after he had gone forth.

rows.pali: Therassa kira adhunāpabbajitassa piṇḍāya carato ekaṃ itthiṃ disvā rāgo uppajjati.
rows.english: As the elder was wandering for alms, it seems, soon after going forth, lust arose in him on seeing a woman.

rows.pali: Tato ānandattheraṃ āha –
rows.english: Thereupon he said to the venerable Ānanda:

rows.pali: Thero āha –
rows.english: The elder said:

rows.pali: Thero rāgaṃ vinodetvā piṇḍāya cari.
rows.english: The elder expelled his lust and then went on with his alms round.

rows.pali: Apica indriyasaṃvarapūrakena bhikkhunā kuraṇḍakamahāleṇavāsinā cittaguttattherena viya corakamahāvihāravāsinā mahāmittattherena viya ca bhavitabbaṃ.
rows.english: 104.Moreover, a bhikkhu who is fulfilling restraint of the faculties should be like the Elder Cittagutta resident in the Great Cave at Kuraṇḍaka, and like the Elder Mahā Mitta resident at the Great Monastery of Coraka.

rows.pali: Kuraṇḍakamahāleṇe kira sattannaṃ buddhānaṃ abhinikkhamanacittakammaṃ manoramaṃ ahosi, sambahulā bhikkhū senāsanacārikaṃ āhiṇḍantā cittakammaṃ disvā "manoramaṃ, bhante, cittakamma"nti āhaṃsu.
rows.english: 105.In the Great Cave of Kuraṇḍaka, it seems, there was a lovely painting of the Renunciation of the Seven Buddhas. A number of bhikkhus wandering about among the dwellings saw the painting and said, “What a lovely painting, venerable sir!

rows.pali: Thero āha "atirekasaṭṭhi me, āvuso, vassāni leṇe vasantassa cittakammaṃ atthītipi na jānāmi, ajja dāni cakkhumante nissāya ñāta"nti.
rows.english: ” The elder said: “For more than sixty years, friends, I have lived in the cave, and I did not know whether there was any painting there or not.

rows.pali: Therena kira ettakaṃ addhānaṃ vasantena cakkhuṃ ummīletvā leṇaṃ na ullokitapubbaṃ.
rows.english: Now, today, I know it through those who have eyes.” The elder, it seems, though he had lived there for so long, had never raised his eyes and looked up at the cave.

rows.pali: Leṇadvāre cassa mahānāgarukkhopi ahosi.
rows.english: And at the door of his cave there was a great ironwood tree.

rows.pali: Sopi therena uddhaṃ na ullokitapubbo.
rows.english: And the elder had never looked up at that either.

rows.pali: Anusaṃvaccharaṃ bhūmiyaṃ kesaranipātaṃ disvāvassa pupphitabhāvaṃ jānāti.
rows.english: He knew it was in flower when he saw its petals on the ground each year.

rows.pali: Rājā therassa guṇasampattiṃ sutvā vanditukāmo tikkhattuṃ pesetvā anāgacchante there tasmiṃ gāme taruṇaputtānaṃ itthīnaṃ thane bandhāpetvā lañjāpesi "tāva dārakā thaññaṃ mā labhiṃsu, yāva thero na āgacchatī"ti.
rows.english: 106. The king heard of the elder’s great virtues, and he sent for him three times, desiring to pay homage to him. When the elder did not go, he had the breasts of all the women with infants in the town bound and sealed off, [saying] “As long as the elder does not come let the children go without milk,”

rows.pali: Thero dārakānaṃ anukampāya mahāgāmaṃ agamāsi.
rows.english: Out of compassion for the children the elder went to Mahāgāma.

rows.pali: Rājā sutvā "gacchatha bhaṇe, theraṃ pavesetha sīlāni gaṇhissāmī"ti antepuraṃ abhiharāpetvā vanditvā bhojetvā "ajja, bhante, okāso natthi, sve sīlāni gaṇhissāmīti therassa pattaṃ gahetvā thokaṃ anugantvā deviyā saddhiṃ vanditvā nivatti.
rows.english: When the king heard [that he had come, he said] “Go and bring the elder in. I shall take the precepts.” Having had him brought up into the inner palace, he paid homage to him and provided him with a meal. Then, saying, “Today, venerable sir, there is no opportunity. I shall take the precepts tomorrow,” he took the elder’s bowl. After following him for a little, he paid homage with the queen and turned back.

rows.pali: Thero rājā vā vandatu devī vā, "sukhī hotu, mahārājā"ti vadati.
rows.english: whether it was the king who paid homage or whether it was the queen, the elder said, “May the king be happy.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ sattadivasā gatā.
rows.english: As seven days went by thus (above).

rows.pali: Bhikkhū āhaṃsu "kiṃ, bhante, tumhe raññepi vandamāne deviyāpi vandamānāya "sukhī hotu, mahārāja"icceva vadathāti.
rows.english: 107. Bhikkhus asked: “Why is it, venerable sir, that whether it is the king who pays the homage or the queen you say ‘May the king be happy’?

rows.pali: Thero "nāhaṃ, āvuso, rājāti vā devīti vā vavatthānaṃ karomī"ti vatvā sattāhātikkamena "therassa idha vāso dukkho"ti raññā vissajjito kuraṇḍakamahāleṇaṃ gantvā rattibhāge caṅkamaṃ ārūhi.
rows.english: ” The elder replied: “Friends, I do not notice whether it is the king or the queen.” At the end of seven days [when it was found that] the elder was not happy living there, he was dismissed by the king. He went back to the Great Cave at Kuraṇḍaka. When it was night he went out onto his walk.

rows.pali: Nāgarukkhe adhivatthā devatā daṇḍadīpikaṃ gahetvā aṭṭhāsi.
rows.english: A deity who dwelt in the ironwood tree stood by with a torch of sticks.

rows.pali: Athassa kammaṭṭhānaṃ atiparisuddhaṃ pākaṭaṃ ahosi.
rows.english: Then his meditation subject became quite clear and plain.

rows.pali: Thero "kiṃ nu me ajja kammaṭṭhānaṃ ativiya pakāsatī"ti attamano majjhimayāmasamanantaraṃ sakalaṃ pabbataṃ unnādayanto arahattaṃ pāpuṇi.
rows.english: The elder, [thinking] “How clear my meditation subject is today! ” was glad, and immediately after the middle watch he reached Arahantship, making the whole rock resound.30

rows.pali: Tasmā aññopi attatthakāmo kulaputto –
rows.english: 108. So when another clansman seeks his own good:

rows.pali: Mahāmittattherassāpi mātu visagaṇḍakarogo uppajji, dhītāpissā bhikkhunīsu pabbajitā hoti.
rows.english: 109. The Elder Mahā Mitta’s mother was sick with a poisoned tumour. She told her daughter, who as a bhikkhunī had also gone forth

rows.pali: Sā taṃ āha – "gaccha ayye, bhātu santikaṃ gantvā mama aphāsukabhāvaṃ ārocetvā bhesajjamāharā"ti.
rows.english: , “Lady, go to your brother. Tell him my trouble and bring back some medicine.”

rows.pali: Sā gantvā ārocesi.
rows.english: She went and told him.

rows.pali: Thero āha – "nāhaṃ mūlabhesajjādīni saṃharitvā bhesajjaṃ pacituṃ jānāmi, apica te bhesajjaṃ ācikkhissaṃ – "ahaṃ yato pabbajito, tato paṭṭhāya na mayā lobhasahagatena cittena indriyāni bhinditvā visabhāgarūpaṃ olokitapubbaṃ, iminā saccavacanena mātuyā me phāsu hotu, gaccha idaṃ vatvā upāsikāya sarīraṃ parimajjā"ti.
rows.english: The elder said: “I do not know how to gather root medicines and such things and concoct a medicine from them. But rather I will tell you a medicine: since I went forth I have not broken [my virtue of restraint of] the sense faculties by looking at the bodily form of the opposite sex with a lustful mind. By this declaration of truth may my mother get well. Go and tell the lay devotee and rub her body.”

rows.pali: Sā gantvā imamatthaṃ ārocetvā tathā akāsi.
rows.english: She went and told her what had happened and then did as she had been instructed.

rows.pali: Upāsikāya taṃkhaṇaṃyeva gaṇḍo pheṇapiṇḍo viya vilīyitvā antaradhāyi, sā uṭṭhahitvā "sace sammāsambuddho dhareyya, kasmā mama puttasadisassa bhikkhuno jālavicitrena hatthena sīsaṃ na parāmaseyyā"ti attamanavācaṃ nicchāresi.
rows.english: At that very moment the lay devotee’s tumour vanished, shrinking away like a lump of froth. She got up and uttered a cry of joy: “If the Fully Enlightened One were still alive, why should he not stroke with his netadorned hand the head of a bhikkhu like my son?”

rows.pali: Tasmā –
rows.english: So:

rows.pali: Yathā pana indriyasaṃvaro satiyā, tathā vīriyena ājīvapārisuddhi sampādetabbā.
rows.english: 111.(c) As restraint of the faculties is to be undertaken by means of mindfulness, so livelihood purification is to be undertaken by means of energy.

rows.pali: Vīriyasādhanā hi sā, sammāraddhavīriyassa micchājīvappahānasambhavato.
rows.english: For that is accomplished by energy, because the abandoning of wrong livelihood is effected in one who has rightly applied energy.

rows.pali: Tasmā anesanaṃ appatirūpaṃ pahāya vīriyena piṇḍapātacariyādīhi sammā esanāhi esā sampādetabbā parisuddhuppādeyeva paccaye paṭisevamānena aparisuddhuppāde āsīvise viya parivajjayatā.
rows.english: Abandoning, therefore, unbefitting wrong search, this should be undertaken with energy by means of the right kind of search consisting in going on alms round, etc., avoiding what is of impure origin as though it were a poisonous snake, and using only requisites of pure origin.

rows.pali: Tattha apariggahitadhutaṅgassa saṅghato, gaṇato, dhammadesanādīhi cassa guṇehi pasannānaṃ gihīnaṃ santikā uppannā paccayā parisuddhuppādā nāma.
rows.english: 112.Herein, for one who has not taken up the ascetic practices, any requisites obtained from the Community, from a group of bhikkhus, or from laymen who have confidence in his special qualities of teaching the Dhamma, etc., are called “of pure origin.”

rows.pali: Piṇḍapātacariyādīhi pana atiparisuddhuppādāyeva.
rows.english: But those obtained on alms round, etc., are of extremely pure origin.

rows.pali: Pariggahitadhutaṅgassa piṇḍapātacariyādīhi dhutaguṇe cassa pasannānaṃ santikā dhutaṅganiyamānulomena uppannā parisuddhuppādā nāma.
rows.english: For one who has taken up the ascetic practices, those obtained on alms round, etc., and—as long as this is in accordance with the rules of the ascetic practices—from people who have confidence in his special qualities of asceticism, are called “of pure origin.”

rows.pali: Ekabyādhivūpasamatthañcassa pūtihariṭakīcatumadhuresu uppannesu "catumadhuraṃ aññepi sabrahmacārino paribhuñjissantī"ti cintetvā hariṭakīkhaṇḍameva paribhuñjamānassa dhutaṅgasamādānaṃ patirūpaṃ hoti.
rows.english: And if he has got putrid urine with mixed gall nuts and “four-sweets”31 for the purpose of curing a certain affliction, and he eats only the broken gall nuts, thinking, “Other companions in the life of purity will eat the ‘four-sweets’,” his undertaking of the ascetic practices is befitting,

rows.pali: Esa hi "uttamaariyavaṃsiko bhikkhū"ti vuccati.
rows.english: for he is then called a bhikkhu who is supreme in the Noble Ones’ heritages (A II 28).

rows.pali: Ye panete cīvarādayo paccayā, tesu yassa kassaci bhikkhuno ājīvaṃ parisodhentassa cīvare ca piṇḍapāte ca nimittobhāsaparikathāviññattiyo na vaṭṭanti.
rows.english: 113. As to the robe and the other requisites, no hint, indication, roundabout talk, or intimation about robes and alms food is allowable for a bhikkhu who is purifying his livelihood.

rows.pali: Senāsane pana apariggahitadhutaṅgassa nimittobhāsaparikathā vaṭṭanti.
rows.english: But a hint, indication, or roundabout talk about a resting place is allowable for one who has not taken up the ascetic practices.

rows.pali: Tattha nimittaṃ nāma senāsanatthaṃ bhūmiparikammādīni karontassa "kiṃ, bhante, kariyati, ko kārāpetī"ti gihīhi vutte "na koci"ti paṭivacanaṃ, yaṃ vā panaññampi evarūpaṃ nimittakammaṃ.
rows.english: 114. Herein, a “hint” is when one who is getting the preparing of the ground, etc., done for the purpose of [making] a resting place is asked, “What is being done, venerable sir? Who is having it done? ” and he replies, “No one”; or any other such giving of hints.

rows.pali: Obhāso nāma "upāsakā tumhe kuhiṃ vasathā"ti.
rows.english: An “indication” is saying, “Lay follower, where do you live?”

rows.pali: Pāsāde, bhanteti.
rows.english: —”In a mansion, venerable sir”

rows.pali: "Bhikkhūnaṃ pana upāsakā pāsādo na vaṭṭatī"ti vacanaṃ, yaṃ vā panaññampi evarūpaṃ obhāsakammaṃ.
rows.english: —”But, lay follower, a mansion is not allowed for bhikkhus.” Or any other such giving of indication.

rows.pali: Parikathā nāma "bhikkhusaṅghassa senāsanaṃ sambādha"nti vacanaṃ, yā vā panaññāpi evarūpā pariyāyakathā.
rows.english: “Roundabout talk” is saying, “The resting place for the Community of Bhikkhus is crowded”; or any other such oblique talk.

rows.pali: Bhesajje sabbampi vaṭṭati.
rows.english: 115. All, however, is allowed in the case of medicine.

rows.pali: Tathā uppannaṃ pana bhesajjaṃ roge vūpasante paribhuñjituṃ vaṭṭati, na vaṭṭatīti.
rows.english: But when the disease is cured, is it or is it not allowed to use the medicine obtained in this way?

rows.pali: Tattha vinayadharā "bhagavatā dvāraṃ dinnaṃ, tasmā vaṭṭatī"ti vadanti.
rows.english: Herein, the Vinaya specialists say that the opening has been given by the Blessed One, therefore it is allowable.

rows.pali: Suttantikā pana "kiñcāpi āpatti na hoti, ājīvaṃ pana kopeti, tasmā na vaṭṭati"cceva vadanti.
rows.english: But the Suttanta specialists say that though there is no offence, nevertheless the livelihood is sullied, therefore it is not allowable.

rows.pali: Yo pana bhagavatā anuññātāpi nimittobhāsaparikathāviññattiyo akaronto appicchatādiguṇeyeva nissāya jīvitakkhayepi paccupaṭṭhite aññatreva obhāsādīhi uppannapaccaye paṭisevati, esa "paramasallekhavuttī"ti vuccati, seyyathāpi thero sāriputto.
rows.english: 116. But one who does not use hints, indications, roundabout talk, or intimation, though these are permitted by the Blessed One, and who depends only on the special qualities of fewness of wishes, etc., and makes use only of requisites obtained otherwise than by indication, etc., even when he thus risks his life, is called supreme in living in effacement, like the venerable Sāriputta.

rows.pali: So kirāyasmā ekasmiṃ samaye pavivekaṃ brūhayamāno mahāmoggallānattherena saddhiṃ aññatarasmiṃ araññe viharati, athassa ekasmiṃ divase udaravātābādho uppajjitvā atidukkhaṃ janesi.
rows.english: 117. It seems that the venerable one was cultivating seclusion at one time, living in a certain forest with the Elder Mahā Moggallāna. One day an affliction of colic arose in him, causing him great pain.

rows.pali: Mahāmoggallānatthero sāyanhasamaye tassāyasmato upaṭṭhānaṃ gato theraṃ nipannaṃ disvā taṃ pavattiṃ pucchitvā "pubbe te, āvuso, kena phāsu hotī"ti pucchi.
rows.english: In the evening the Elder Mahā Moggallāna went to attend upon him. Seeing him lying down, he asked what the reason was. And then he asked, “What used to make you better formerly, friend?”

rows.pali: Thero āha, "gihikāle me, āvuso, mātā sappimadhusakkarādīhi yojetvā asambhinnakhīrapāyāsaṃ adāsi, tena me phāsu ahosī"ti.
rows.english: The elder said, “When I was a layman, friend, my mother used to mix ghee, honey, sugar and so on, and give me rice gruel with pure milk. That used to make me better.”

rows.pali: Sopi āyasmā "hotu, āvuso, sace mayhaṃ vā tuyhaṃ vā puññaṃ atthi, appeva nāma sve labhissāmā"ti āha.
rows.english: Then the other said, “So be it, friend. If either you or I have merit, perhaps tomorrow we shall get some.”

rows.pali: Imaṃ pana nesaṃ kathāsallāpaṃ caṅkamanakoṭiyaṃ rukkhe adhivatthā devatā sutvā "sve ayyassa pāyāsaṃ uppādessāmī"ti tāvadeva therassa upaṭṭhākakulaṃ gantvā jeṭṭhaputtassa sarīraṃ āvisitvā pīḷaṃ janesi.
rows.english: 118. Now, a deity who dwelt in a tree at the end of the walk overheard their conversation. [Thinking] “I will find rice gruel for the lord tomorrow,” he went meanwhile to the family who was supporting the elder and entered into the body of the eldest son, causing him discomfort.

rows.pali: Athassa tikicchānimittaṃ sannipatite ñātake āha – "sace sve therassa evarūpaṃ nāma pāyāsaṃ paṭiyādetha, taṃ muñcissāmī"ti.
rows.english: Then he told the assembled relatives the price of the cure: “If you prepare rice gruel of such a kind tomorrow for the elder, I will set this one free.”

rows.pali: Te "tayā avuttepi mayaṃ therānaṃ nibaddhaṃ bhikkhaṃ demā"ti vatvā dutiyadivase tathārūpaṃ pāyāsaṃ paṭiyādiyiṃsu.
rows.english: They said: “Even without being told by you we regularly supply the elder’s needs,” and on the following day they prepared rice gruel of the kind needed.

rows.pali: Mahāmoggallānatthero pātova āgantvā "āvuso, yāva ahaṃ piṇḍāya caritvā āgacchāmi, tāva idheva hohī"ti vatvā gāmaṃ pāvisi.
rows.english: 119.The Elder Mahā Moggallāna came in the morning and said, “Stay here, friend, till I come back from the alms round.” Then he went into the village.

rows.pali: Te manussā paccuggantvā therassa pattaṃ gahetvā vuttappakārassa pāyāsassa pūretvā adaṃsu.
rows.english: Those people met him. They took his bowl, filled it with the stipulated kind of rice gruel, and gave it back to him.

rows.pali: Thero gamanākāraṃ dassesi.
rows.english: The elder made as though to go,

rows.pali: Te "bhuñjatha – bhante, tumhe, aparampi dassāmā"ti theraṃ bhojetvā puna pattapūraṃ adaṃsu.
rows.english: but they said, “Eat, venerable sir, we shall give you more.” When the elder had eaten, they gave him another bowlful.

rows.pali: Thero gantvā "handāvuso sāriputta, paribhuñjā"ti upanāmesi.
rows.english: The elder left. Bringing the alms food to the venerable Sāriputta, he said, “Here, friend Sāriputta, eat.”

rows.pali: Theropi taṃ disvā "atimanāpo pāyāso, kathaṃ nu kho uppanno"ti cintento tassa uppattimūlaṃ disvā āha – "āvuso moggallāna, aparibhogāraho piṇḍapāto"ti.
